questions.question: The isoelectronic set of ions is
questions.topicName: Kossel-Lewis Approach to Chemical Bonding
questions.explanation:
The species with its atomic number and number of electrons are as follows:

Species (ions) At. no. (Z) No. of electrons
N3- 7 7 + 3 = 10
O2- 8 8 + 2 = 10
F- 9 9 + 1 = 10
Na+ 11 11 - 1 = 10
Li+ 3 3 - 1 = 2
Mg2+ 12 12 - 2 = 10

Thus, option (N3-, O2-, F- and Na+) contains isoelectronic set of ions.

questions.question: The intermolecular interaction that is dependent on the inverse cube of the distance between the molecules is
questions.topicName: Kossel-Lewis Approach to Chemical Bonding
questions.explanation:
Ion-ion interaction is dependent on the square of the distance, i.e. ion-ion interaction {tex}\propto \frac{1}{r^{2}}{/tex}
Similarly, ion-dipole interaction {tex}\propto \frac{1}{r^{3}}{/tex}
London force and dipole-dipole interaction {tex}\propto \frac{1}{r^{3}}{/tex}
Superficially it seems as both ion-dipole interaction and hydrogen bonding vary with the inverse cube of the distance between the molecules but when we look at the exact expressions of field (force) created in two situations, it comes as
{tex}|E| \text { or }|F|=\frac{2|P|}{4 \pi \in r^{3}}{/tex} (In case of ion-dipole interaction)
and {tex}F=\frac{2 q^{2} r-4 q^{2} a}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0} r^{3}}{/tex} (In case of dipole-dipole interaction)
From the above, it is clear that the ion-dipole interaction is the better answer as compared to dipole-dipole interaction, i.e. hydrogen bonding.

questions.question: In {tex}\mathrm{PO}_{4}^{3-}{/tex} ion, the formal charge on each oxygen atom and P-O bond order respectively are:
questions.topicName: Kossel-Lewis Approach to Chemical Bonding
questions.explanation:
The total charge = -3
So the average formal charge on each ‘O’ atom is {tex}\frac{-3}{4}{/tex} = -0.75
Again total no. of electrons in the valence shell of {tex}\mathrm{PO}_{4}^{3-}{/tex} ion = 5 + 8 = 13
No. of electrons involved in bond formation in {tex}\mathrm{PO}_{4}^{3-}{/tex} ion = 13 - 3 = 10
No. of bonds in {tex}\mathrm{PO}_{4}^{3-}=\frac{10}{2}=5{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Average P-O bond order {tex}=\frac{5}{4}=1.25{/tex}

questions.question:
Which of the following compounds readily decomposes on heating to give ionic compound with higher value of lattice energy as compare to corresponding reactant?

  4. Na2O2

{tex}\mathrm{BeSO}_{4} \stackrel{\Delta}{\longrightarrow} \mathrm{BeO}+\mathrm{SO}_{2}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}{/tex}

In all of the above reactions, the lattice energy of the product is higher as compared to the reactant.

questions.question:
Two elements A and B have electronic configurations:
A = 1s2 2s2 2p6 3s2
B = 1s2 2s2 2p6 3s2 3p5
The expected compound formed by the combination of A and B is ________.
questions.topicName: Ionic Bonding
questions.explanation: Element A donates 2e- and forms a stable cation A2+ ({tex}\because{/tex} octet is complete). Element B requires 1e- to complete its octet. Hence, the expected compound formed by the combination of A and B should be AB2.

questions.question: Find the difference in percent ionic character between HCl and HBr. The observed dipole moment is 1.03 D for HCl and 0.82 D for HBr. Bond length of HCl is 1.275{tex}\mathop A \limits^{\circ}{/tex} and HBr is 1.41{tex}\mathop A \limits^{\circ}{/tex}. Charge = 4.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 esu.
questions.topicName: Ionic Bonding
questions.explanation:
If HCl and HBr were 100% ionic, each would carry charge equal to one unit, i.e. 4.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 esu.
For HCl : {tex}\mu_{ionic}{/tex} = Q {tex}\times{/tex} r
= 4.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 esu {tex}\times{/tex} 1.275 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 cm
= 6.12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-18 esu cm
= 6.12 D
For HBr: {tex}\mu_{ionic}{/tex} = q {tex}\times{/tex} d
= 4.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 esu {tex}\times{/tex} 1.41 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8
= 6.768 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-18 esu cm
= 6.77 D
% ionic character (HCl) = {tex}\frac{\mu_{\text {observed }}}{\mu_{\text {theoretical }}} \times 100{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1.03}{6.12} \times 100{/tex}
= 16.83%
% ionic character (HBr) = {tex}\frac{\mu_{\text {observed }}}{\mu_{\text {theoretical }}} \times 100{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{0.82}{6.77} \times 100{/tex}
= 12.11%
The difference in percentage ionic character
= {tex}\mu_{\text {ionic }}({HCl})-\mu_{\text {ionic }}({HBr}){/tex}
= 16.83 - 12.11
= 4.72

questions.question: Outer electronic configuration of four elements are given below. Which one among these has the greatest tendency to form an electrovalent bond?
questions.topicName: Ionic Bonding
questions.explanation: According to the electronic configuration in 2s2 2p5, the element requires only one electron to complete its octet state. Hence it will have the greatest tendency to accept an electron and form an electrovalent bond (ionic bond).

questions.question: In a covalent bond, a difference of 1.7 in the electronegativities of the combining atoms produces an ionic character of about ________.
questions.topicName: Ionic Bonding
questions.explanation: As a general rule, if the electronegativity difference between the two combining atoms is more than 1.7, the bond will have an ionic character. If the electronegativity difference is between 0.4 and 1.7, the bond will have a polar covalent character. If the electronegativity difference is less than 0.4, the bond will have a nonpolar covalent character. Therefore, if the electronegativity difference is 1.7, the bond will have approximately 50% ionic character.

questions.question: In NO-3 ion, the number of bond pairs and lone pairs of electrons on nitrogen atom are
questions.topicName: Covalent Bonding
questions.explanation:
The nitrate ion is formed by the loss of the hydrogen ion, and so its structure is:

Around the central nitrogen there are 4 pairs of shared electrons, and no remaining lone pair. The original lone pair has now become a bonding pair. Two of those pairs make up a double bond. The double bond unit and the two single bonds arrange themselves as far apart as possible in a trigonal planar arrangement - exactly the same as the carbonate ion.

questions.question: Among the following, the maximum covalent character is shown by the compound is:
questions.topicName: Covalent Bonding
questions.explanation:
Few ionic bonds have partial covalent characteristics which were first discussed by a scientist, Kazimierz Fajans in 1923. He gave the rule to predict whether a chemical bond will be covalent or ionic which is now commonly known as Fajan’s rule. The rule can be stated as:

  • Size of the ion: Smaller the size of cation, larger the size of anion, greater is the covalent character of the ionic bond.
  • Charge of Cation: Greater the charge of cation, greater is the covalent character of the ionic bond.
  • Electronic configuration- For cations with the same charge and size, the one, with (n-1)dnns​​​0 which is found in transition elements, has greater covalent character than the cation with ns2np6 electronic configuration, which is commonly found in alkali or alkaline earth metals.

Keeping these rules in mind, we can say that AlCl​​​3 has a high covalent character.

The molecule of CHCl3 contains three chlorine atoms which are highly electronegative and have strong Dipole-dipole attractions. Hence, it is highly polar.
CCl4 molecule has a uniform partial negative charge in all directions. Hence, it is non-polar.
The molecule of CCl4 is also bigger than CHCl3 so it has a strong dispersion force which leads to a higher boiling point than CHCl3.

questions.question:
Anhydrous AlCl3 is covalent. From the data given below:
Lattice energy = 5137 KJ/mol.
{tex}\Delta H{/tex} hydration for Al3+ = -4665 KJ/mol
{tex}\Delta H{/tex} hydration for Cl- = -381 KJ/mol
Identify the correct statement.

options.option: The solution will consist of hydrated Al^3+ and Cl^-
The solution will not consists of Al^2+ and Cl^-
It will remain covalent in aqueous solution
The solution will consists of Al^3+ and Cl^-

questions.topicName: Covalent Bonding
questions.explanation:
{tex}\Delta H_{\text {Hydration }}{/tex} = -4665 - 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 381 = -5808
{tex}\left|\Delta H_{\text {Hydration }}\right|>\left|\Delta H_{\mathrm{LE}}\right|{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \Delta H_{\text {solution }}{/tex} < 0
Hence, AlCl3 will dissolve completely in water forming hydrated ions.

questions.question: Among LiCl, BeCl2, BCl3 and CCl4, the covalent bond character follows the order:
questions.topicName: Covalent Bonding
questions.explanation:
According to Fajans' rule,
covalent character {tex}\propto \frac {1}{\text {size of cation}}{/tex}
Order of size of cation is Li > Be > B > C
Thus, the order of covalent character is LiCl < BeCl2 < BCl3 < CCl4.

questions.question: Arrange the following compounds in increasing order of their covalent character.
questions.topicName: Covalent Bonding
questions.explanation:
As the difference in electronegativity between two atoms increases, the bond becomes more polar, i.e., covalent character decreases and ionic character increases. Ca is an electropositive metal. Hence, its electronegativity is very low. The electronegativity value of halogens is in the order, F > Cl > Br > I.
Thus, the correct order of increasing covalent character is CaF2 < CaCl2 < CaBr2 < CaI2.

topics.topicName: Dipole Moment: VSEPR theory and Shapes of Simple Molecules

questions.question: The product of the magnitude of the charge and the distance between the centres of positive and negative charge is called _______.
questions.topicName: Dipole Moment: VSEPR theory and Shapes of Simple Molecules
questions.explanation: A dipole moment ​is a measurement of the separation of two opposite electrical charges. Dipole moments are a vector quantity. The magnitude is equal to the charge multiplied by the distance between the charges and the direction is from negative charge to positive charge: {tex}\mu{/tex} = q {tex}\times{/tex} r

questions.question: The valence shell electron pair repulsion (VSEPR) theory helps in the:

questions.topicName: Dipole Moment: VSEPR theory and Shapes of Simple Molecules
questions.explanation: Valence shell electron pair repulsion (VSEPR) theory is a model used in chemistry to predict the geometry of individual molecules from the number of electron pairs surrounding their central atoms. It is also named the Gillespie-Nyholm theory after its two main developers.

questions.topicName: Dipole Moment: VSEPR theory and Shapes of Simple Molecules
questions.explanation:
Presence of a bond pair and lone pair affects the shape of the molecule. This is because lone pairs and bond pairs repel each other and ultimately change the shape of the molecule.
BCl3 has 3 bond pairs and 0 lone pair.
NCl3 has 3 bond pairs and 1 lone pair.

questions.question: Which among the following has the largest dipole moment?
questions.topicName: Dipole Moment: VSEPR theory and Shapes of Simple Molecules
questions.explanation: There is a bond dipole moment pointing from each H to the O, making the oxygen partially negative and the hydrogen partially positive. In addition, the O also has non-bonded electrons in the direction opposite the hydrogen atoms. There is thus a large molecular dipole, pointing from a positive region between the two hydrogen atoms to the negative region of the oxygen atom. The charge differences cause water molecules to be attracted to each other (the relatively positive areas being attracted to the relatively negative areas) and to other polar molecules. For other compounds, the dipole moment is not that significant when compared to the water molecule.

In a dichromate ion (Cr2​O7−2​), there are 6 terminal Cr – O bonds. All these bonds are equivalent due to resonance.
The dichromate ions (Cr2​O7−2​) are oxoanions of chromium in the oxidation state +6.

questions.question: The statement true for {tex}{N_3}^ - {/tex} is

options.option: The formal oxidation state of nitrogen in this anion is -1

questions.topicName: Dipole Moment: VSEPR theory and Shapes of Simple Molecules
questions.explanation:
The azide ion (N3-) has 16 valence electrons and is isoelectronic with CO2. The N3- ion is linear (N−N−N)-like CO2molecule. The formal oxidation state of nitrogen in this anion is −1 because the net charge on the ion is −1 as shown in the following resonating structures.

The (N3)− ion is considered as a pseudohalide ion. It forms the extremely unstable and explosive compounds as fluorazide FN3, chlorazide CIN3, bromazide BrN3, and iodazide IN3, but the dimer N3−N3 is unknown.

questions.question: H2O is dipolar, whereas BeF2 is not. It is because:

options.option: H_2O is linear and BeF_2 is angular
H_2O is angular and BeF_2 is linear
H_2O involves hydrogen bonding whereas BeF_2 is a discrete molecule
the electronegativity of F is greater than that of O

questions.topicName: Dipole Moment: VSEPR theory and Shapes of Simple Molecules
questions.explanation: The overall value of the dipole moment of a polar molecule depends on its geometry and shape, i.e., vectorial addition of dipole moment of the constituent bonds. Water has angular structure with bond angle 105° as it has dipole moment. However, BeF2 is a linear molecule since dipole moment summation of all the bonds present in the molecule cancel each other.

questions.question: Among the following, the pair in which the two species are not isostructural is:
questions.topicName: Dipole Moment: VSEPR theory and Shapes of Simple Molecules
questions.explanation:
SiF4 has symmetrical tetrahedral shape which is due to sp3 hybridisation of the central silicon atom in its excited state configuration.
SF4 has distorted tetrahedral or sea-saw geometry which arises due to sp3d hybridisation of central sulphur atom and due to the presence of one lone pair of electrons in one of the equatorial hybrid orbital.

questions.question: Which of the following is not isostructural with SiCl4?
questions.topicName: Dipole Moment: VSEPR theory and Shapes of Simple Molecules
questions.explanation: SCl4, {tex}\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}, \mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}{/tex} and {tex}\mathrm{PO}_{4}^{3-}{/tex} ions are the examples of molecules/ions which are of AB4 type and have tetrahedral structure. SCl4 is AB4(lone pair) types species. Although the arrangement of five sp3d hybrid orbitals in space is trigonal bipyramidal, due to the presence of one lone pair of electron in the basal hybrid orbital, the shape of AB4 (lone pair) species gets distorted and becomes distorted tetrahedral or see-saw.

questions.question: Which of the following pairs of compounds is isoelectronic and isostructural?
questions.topicName: Dipole Moment: VSEPR theory and Shapes of Simple Molecules
questions.explanation: {tex}\mathrm{IBr}_{2}^{-1}{/tex} and XeF2 are iso-structural and both C.A. consist of same no. of valence e-s.

questions.question:
Predict the correct order among the following:

  1. bond pair-bond pair > lone pair-bond pair > lone pair-lone pair
  2. lone pair-bond pair > bond pair-bond pair > lone pair-lone pair
  3. lone pair-lone pair > lone pair-bond pair > bond pair-bond pair
  4. lone pair-lone pair > bond pair-bond pair > lone pair-bond pair
questions.topicName: Dipole Moment: VSEPR theory and Shapes of Simple Molecules
questions.explanation: According to VSEPR theory, the repulsive forces between lone pair and lone pair are greater than between lone pair and bond pair which are further greater than bond pair and bond pair.

questions.question:
Match the interhalogen compounds of column-I with the geometry in column-II and assign the correct code:

Column I Column II
(a) XX' (i) T-shape
(b) XX'3 (ii) Pentagonal bipyramidal
(c) XX'5 (iii) Linear
(d) XX'7 (iv) Square-pyramidal
  (v) Tetrahedral

Code:
questions.topicName: Dipole Moment: VSEPR theory and Shapes of Simple Molecules
questions.explanation:
XX' {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Linear
XX'3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} T-shape (sp3d)
XX'5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Square pyramidal (sp3d2)
XX'7 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Pentagonal bipyramidal (sp3d3)

questions.question:
Consider the skeleton structure of {tex}\mathrm{HSO}_{5}^{-}{/tex} ion.

The correct order of w, x, y and z is:
questions.topicName: Dipole Moment: VSEPR theory and Shapes of Simple Molecules
questions.explanation:
In the given structure

Bigger atoms have less extent of overlapping, hence, have longer bond length thus
S—O > O—O > O—H
z bonds of S—O have more double bond character than Y bond of S—O due to resonance, so, order of bond length is:
y > z > x > w

questions.question:
Which of the following is not a correct statement?

  1. Multiple bonds are always shorter than corresponding single bonds.
  2. The electron-deficient molecules can act as Lewis acids.
  3. The canonical structures have no real existence.
  4. Every AB5 molecule does in fact have square pyramid structure.
questions.topicName: Dipole Moment: VSEPR theory and Shapes of Simple Molecules
questions.explanation:
For AB5 molecules, there are three possible geometries i.e., planar pentagonal, square pyramidal and trigonal bipyramidal.

Out of these three geometries, it is only trigonal pyramidal shape in which bond pair-bond pair repulsions are minimum and hence this geometry is the most probable geometry of AB5 molecule.

questions.question: In which of the following all fluorine atoms are not in the same plane
questions.topicName: Dipole Moment: VSEPR theory and Shapes of Simple Molecules
questions.explanation:
• {tex}{SF}_4{/tex} (Sulfur tetrafluoride) has a seesaw molecular geometry, where the fluorine atoms are not all in the same plane. This occurs due to the presence of a lone pair of electrons, which causes a non-planar arrangement.
  • The other compounds {tex}\left({XeF}_4, {C}_2 {~F}_4\right.{/tex}, and {tex}{XeOF}_4{/tex}) have planar or symmetrical structures, where all the fluorine atoms lie in the same plane.

questions.question:
The CORRECT order of boiling point is:

  1. BF3 < B(C2H5)3, NF3 > N(C2H5)3, BiF3 > Bi(C2H5)3
  2. BF3 > B(C2H5)3, N(C2H5)3 > NF3, BiF3 > Bi(C2H5)3
  3. BF3 < B(C2H5)3, NF3 < N(C2H5)3, BiF3 > Bi(C2H5)3
  4. BF3 < B(C2H5)3, NF3 < N(C2H5)3, BiF3 < Bi(C2H5)3
questions.topicName: Dipole Moment: VSEPR theory and Shapes of Simple Molecules
questions.explanation:
BF3 < B(C2H5)3; due to molecular weight
NF3 < N(C2H5)3; due to molecular weight
BiF3 > Bi(C2Hs)3; due to BiF3 is ionic compound
So lattice energy is high and Bi(C2H5)3 is covalent compound.

questions.question: If AB4 molecule is a polar molecule, a possible geometry of AB4 is:
questions.topicName: Dipole Moment: VSEPR theory and Shapes of Simple Molecules
questions.explanation:
For AB4 compound possible geometry are

No. of Bond pair No. of lone pair Hybridisation
4 0 sp3
4 1 sp3d
4 2 sp3d2

Structure with sp3d2 hybridization is polar due to lone pair moment while in other possibilities molecules are non-polar.
Square pyramidal can be polar due to lone pair moment as the bond pair moments will get cancelled out.

questions.question: The shape/structure of [XeF5]- and XeO3F2, respectively, are:
questions.topicName: Dipole Moment: VSEPR theory and Shapes of Simple Molecules
questions.explanation:
1. {tex}\mathrm{XeF}_{5}^{-}{/tex}
    St. No. = Bond pair + Lone Pair
    = (5 + 2) = 7
    So, hybridisation is = sp3d3
    and structure is pentagonal planar.
  2. XeO3F2
    St. No. = 5
    So, hybridisation is = sp3d
    and structure is trigonal bipyramidal.

questions.question: The incorrect geometry is represented by
questions.topicName: Dipole Moment: VSEPR theory and Shapes of Simple Molecules
questions.explanation: NF3 has trigonal pyramidal geometry. N atom has one lone pair and three bond pairs of electrons. The electron pair geometry is tetrahedral and molecular geometry is trigonal pyramidal. The bond angles are lower than tetrahedral bond angles due to lone pair - lone pair and lone pair-bond pair repulsions. N atom is sp3 hybridized.

questions.question: The compound with no dipole moment is
questions.topicName: Dipole Moment: VSEPR theory and Shapes of Simple Molecules
questions.explanation:
TIPS/Formulae:

  1. Dipole moment is vector quantity. When vector sum of all dipoles in molecule will be zero, then molecule will not have net dipole moment.
  2. NOTE: Fornet dipole moment to be equal to zero, all the atoms attached to central atom must be identical and geometry must be regular.

    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Carbon tetrachloride having regular geometry and identical atoms attached to bonds has zero dipole moment.

SF4
Bond pair = 4
Lone pair = 1
Steric number = 5,
So, hybridisation is sp3d.

Geometry is trigonal bipyramidal but the shape is "See-Saw",

questions.question:
Predict the CORRECT order among the following:

  1. bond pair - bond pair > lone pair - bond pair > lone pair - lone pair
  2. lone pair - bond pair > bond pair - bond pair > lone pair - lone pair
  3. lone pair - lone pair > lone pair - bond pair > bond pair - bond pair
  4. lone pair - lone pair > bond pair - bond pair > lone pair - bond pair
questions.topicName: Dipole Moment: VSEPR theory and Shapes of Simple Molecules
questions.explanation:
The forces of repulsion between electron pairs decrease in the following order:
lone pair - lone pair > lone pair - bond pair > bond pair - bond pair

questions.question: According to VSEPR theory, if two bond pairs and no lone pair are present around the central atom, then the geometry of the molecule is ________.

questions.question: Find the ratio of partial positive charge on H-atom in HCl to that in HI, if dipole moment of HCl and HI are 1.03 D and 0.38 D, respectively. Bond length of HCl and HI are 1.27{tex}\mathop A \limits ^{\circ}{/tex} and 1.61{tex}\mathop A \limits ^{\circ}{/tex}, respectively.
questions.topicName: Dipole Moment: VSEPR theory and Shapes of Simple Molecules
questions.explanation:
Q = {tex}\frac{\mu}{r}{/tex}
For HCl = Q = {tex}\frac{1.03 \times 10^{-18} \text { esu cm }}{1.27 \times 10^{-8} {cm}}{/tex}
= 0.811 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 esu
For Hl = Q = {tex}\frac{0.38 \times 10^{-18} \text { esu cm }}{1.61 \times 10^{-8} {cm}}{/tex}
= 0.236 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 esu
Fractional charge ({tex}\delta{/tex}) = {tex}\frac{Q}{e}{/tex}
For HCl {tex}\delta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.811 \times 10^{-10} {esu}}{4.8 \times 10^{-10} {esu}}{/tex} = 0.169
For Hl {tex}\delta{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.236 \times 10^{-10} {esu}}{4.8 \times 10^{-10} {esu}}{/tex} = 0.0492
Ratio {tex}\frac{\delta_{{HCl}}}{\delta_{{Hl}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.169}{0.0492}{/tex} = 3.42
{tex}\delta_{{HCl}}: \delta_{{HI}}{/tex} {tex}\equiv{/tex} 3.43 : 1

questions.question:
Which of the following is INCORRECT?

  1. CHBr3 is higher than dipole moment of CH2Br2.
  2. Dipole moment of o-dichlorobenzene is higher than dipole moment of p-dichlorobenzene.
  3. Dipole moment of trans-but-2-ene is zero.
  4. Dipole moment of trans-1-bromopropene is greater than zero.
questions.topicName: Dipole Moment: VSEPR theory and Shapes of Simple Molecules
questions.explanation: In CHBr3, the resultant of two C - Br dipoles is opposed by the resultant of C - H and C - Br dipoles, which is smaller than the former. Therefore, CHBr3 shows non-zero dipole moment. In CH2Br2, the resultant of two C - Br dipoles is reinforced by two C - H bonds. Therefore, CH2Br2 shows non-zero dipole moment, which is higher than that of CHBr3.

Steps {tex}\mathrm{ClO}_{4}^{-}{/tex} ion
Step 1 Lewis structure of {tex}\mathrm{ClO}_{4}^{-}{/tex} ion is:
Step 2 No. of lone pairs on Cl-atom = 0
No. of bonding pairs on Cl-atom = 4
Step 3 There are three electron pairs around the Cl-atom. Therefore, electron pair geometry is tetrahedral.

questions.question:
Find the INCORRECT statement.

  1. NH3 has higher dipole moment than NF3.
  2. CH3OH has higher dipole moment than CH3SH.
  3. CHCI3 has higher dipole moment than CCl4.
  4. BF3 has higher dipole moment than H2O.
questions.topicName: Dipole Moment: VSEPR theory and Shapes of Simple Molecules
questions.explanation:
1. NH3 has higher dipole moment ({tex}\mu{/tex} = 1.47 D) than NF3 ({tex}\mu{/tex} = 0.24 D)
  2. In {tex}\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathop{\mbox{O}}\limits_{\bullet \bullet}^{\bullet \bullet}-{H}{/tex}, the central oxygen atom is more electronegative than sulphur atom. Hence the net dipole moment is more in CH3OH than CH3SH.
  3. CCI4 is symmetrical, non-polar molecule while CHCl3 is an unsymmetrical, polar molecule. Hence CHCl3 has a higher dipole moment.
  4. BF3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\mu{/tex} = 0 (trigonal planar)
    H2O {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\mu{/tex} = 1.85 (bent with 2 lone pairs).
    Hence H2O has higher dipole moment than BF3.

questions.question: Bond order of C - O bond in {tex}CO_{3}^{2-}{/tex} ion is ________.
questions.topicName: Dipole Moment: VSEPR theory and Shapes of Simple Molecules
questions.explanation:
Bond order = {tex}\frac{\text {Total no. of bonds between two atoms in all structures}}{\text {Total no. of resonating structures}}{/tex}
In carbonate ion,

Bond order = {tex}\frac{\text {double bond+ single bond + single bond}}{\text {3}}{/tex}
Bond order = {tex}\frac{{2 + 1 + 1}}{{3}}{/tex} = 1.33

Statement I: Dipole moment is a vector quantity and by convention it is depicted by a small arrow with tail on the negative centre and head pointing towards the positive centre.
Statement II: The crossed arrow of the dipole moment symbolizes the direction of the shift of charges in the molecules.

options.option: Both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct
Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect

questions.explanation:

Statement (II) is incorrect

questions.question: Bond angle helps us in:

options.option: determining the stability of the element
determining the reactivity of the element

questions.topicName: Bond Parameters
questions.explanation: Bond angles also contribute to the shape of a molecule. Bond angles are the angles between adjacent lines representing bonds. The bond angle can help differentiate between linear, trigonal planar, tetrahedral, trigonal-bipyramidal, and octahedral. The ideal bond angles are the angles that demonstrate the maximum angle where it would minimize repulsion, thus verifying the VSEPR theory.

questions.question: The amount of energy required to break one mole of bonds of a particular type between two atoms in a gaseous state is called ________.
questions.topicName: Bond Parameters
questions.explanation: Bond enthalpy also known as bond energy is the energy that is needed to break a particular bond in a gaseous compound. The unit that expresses bond enthalpy is kilojoules per mole or kJ/mol.

questions.question: Which one of the following pairs of species has the same bond order?
questions.topicName: Bond Parameters

questions.question: Rank the bonds in the set C=O, C-O, C {tex} \equiv {/tex} O in order of decreasing bond length.
questions.topicName: Bond Parameters
questions.explanation: The length of the bond is determined by the number of bonded electrons (the bond order). The higher the bond order, the stronger the pull between the two atoms and the shorter the bond length. Generally, the length of the bond between two atoms is approximately the sum of the covalent radii of the two atoms. CO has a triple bond so has minimum bond length.

questions.question: Rank the bonds in the set C=O, C-O, C{tex} \equiv {/tex}O in order of decreasing bond strength.
questions.topicName: Bond Parameters
questions.explanation: The more bonds between atoms, the shorter the bond is. For example, a triple bond will be shorter than a double bond. The shorter the bond is the stronger it is (higher dissociation energy). Bond order is basically the number of bonds between atoms, so a larger number bond order=more bonds=shorter bonds=stronger bonds. i.e., C{tex} \equiv {/tex}O > C=O > C-O.

questions.question: In compounds of type ECl3, where E = B, P, As or Bi, the angles Cl—E—Cl is in order
questions.topicName: Bond Parameters
questions.explanation: When E = B in BCl3,bond angle is 120°. When E = P, As or Bi in ECl3, hybridisation at E will be sp3. Also, if central atoms are from same group, bond angle decreases down the group provided all other things are similar. Hence, the order of bond angles is BCl3 > PCl3 > AsCl3 > BiCl3

questions.question: The correct order of increasing C—O bond length of CO, {tex}\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}{/tex}, CO2 is
questions.topicName: Bond Parameters
questions.explanation:
Bond length {tex}\propto \frac{1}{\text { Bond order }}{/tex}
Bond order : CO, = 2, CO = 3, {tex}\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}=1+\frac{1}{3}=\frac{4}{3}{/tex}
Therefore, order of bond length is {tex}\mathrm{CO}_{3}^{2-}<\mathrm{CO}_{2}<\mathrm{CO}{/tex}

questions.question: Which of the following molecule has highest bond energy?
questions.topicName: Bond Parameters
questions.explanation:
As all the given molecules are bonded together through a single bond. And, more is the bond order of a molecule more closely its atoms will be bonded together. As a result, more strong will the bonding between its combining atoms.
Moreover, there will be the presence of a lone pair of electrons on fluorine, nitrogen, and oxygen atoms. Hence, there will occur lone pair-lone pair repulsion between F-F, N-N, and O-O molecules. Due to which there will occur an increase in its bond length and a decrease in bond energy.
Therefore, only the C-C molecule will have high bond energy as there are no lone pairs present on the carbon atom.

questions.question:
H - N --- N --- N
In hydrogen azide, the bond orders of bonds (I) and (II) are ________.
questions.topicName: Bond Parameters
questions.explanation:
As in the resonance structure of hydrogen azide, it can be seen that number of N - N bond for bond (I) {tex}\leq{/tex} 2.
{tex}{\text{H}} - \mathop {\mathop {\text{N}}\limits^{..} }\limits_{..} \mathop = \limits^{{\text{(I)}}} {{\text{N}}^ + }\mathop = \limits^{({\text{II}})} \mathop {\mathop {\text{N}}\limits^{\overline {..} } }\limits_{..} \leftrightarrow {\text{H}} - {\mathop {\mathop {\text{N}}\limits^{\overline {..} } }\limits_{..} ^{({\text{I}})}}\mathop {\text{N}}\limits^ + \mathop \equiv \limits^{({\text{II}})} \mathop {\text{N}}\limits^{..} {/tex}
Hence for bond (I), bond order will be < 2 whereas for bond (II), number of bond {tex}\ge{/tex} 2. Thus its bond order will be > 2.

questions.question: What is the approximate bond angle between H-O-C in the following compound? The compound has formula HOCH2CH2NH2.
questions.topicName: Bond Parameters
questions.explanation:
The easiest way to determine the hybridization of a carbon, oxygen or nitrogen atom is to look at the number of {tex}\pi{/tex} bonds it forms.
If it forms no {tex}\pi{/tex} bonds, it is sp3 hybridized; if it forms one {tex}\pi{/tex} bond, it is sp2 hybridized; if it forms two {tex}\pi{/tex} bonds, it is sp hybridized.
‘O’ atom undergoes sp3 hybridization. Hence, the approximate bond angle is 104.5o.

questions.question: Which one of the following pairs of species have the same bond order ?
questions.topicName: Bond Parameters
questions.explanation:
For any species to have same bond order we can expect them to have same number of electrons.
Calculating the number of electrons in various species:
CO = (6 + 8 = 14)
NO = (7 + 8 = 15)
O2 = (8 + 8 = 16)
NO+ = (7 + 8 - 1 = 14)
CN- = (6 + 7 + 1 = 14)
O-2 = (8 + 8 + 1 = 17)
N2 = (6 + 7 - 1 = 12)
CN- and CO have same number of electrons and have same bond order equal to 3.

{tex}=\frac{198}{2}{/tex} = 99 pm

But each H2O molecule is linked to four other H2O molecules through H-bonds whereas each HF molecule is linked only to two other HF molecules.
Hence, b.p of H2O > b.p of HF > b.p. of NH3

questions.question: Bond distance in HF is 9.17 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-11m. Dipole moment of HF is 6.104 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-30 Cm. The percentage ionic character in HF will be: (electron charge = 1.60 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 C)
questions.topicName: Bond Parameters
questions.explanation:
Given e = 1.60 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 C; d = 9.17 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-11 m
From {tex}\mu{/tex} = e {tex}\times{/tex} d
{tex}\mu{/tex} = 1.60 {tex}\times{/tex} I0-19 {tex}\times{/tex} 9.17 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-11 = 14.672 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-30
% ionic character = {tex}\frac {\text {Observed dipole moment}}{\text {Dipole moment for 100% ionic bond}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{6.104 \times 10^{-30}}{14.672 \times 10^{-30}} \times 100=41.5 \%{/tex}

questions.topicName: Bond Parameters
questions.explanation:
1. NF3 has trigonal pyramidal shape.
  2. Bond order {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} N2 > O2
    Bond length {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} N2 < O2
  3. Isoelectronic species have same bond order due to same total no. of electrons.
  4. Dipole moment H2O > H2S
    Due to Electronegativity difference.

questions.question: The correct order of C-O bond length among CO, CO32-, CO2 is:
questions.topicName: Bond Parameters
questions.explanation:
Bond length between two atoms decreases from single bond to multiple bonds between them. In CO, there is triple bond between C and O, in CO2 there is double bond between C and O. Thus, bond length of CO2 is greater than CO. In CO32-, there are two single bonds between C and O and third oxygen atom forms double bond with carbon. These three oxygen atoms are identical due to resonance thus, each C-O bond length is smaller than a single bond but greater than the double bond. Thus, the correct order of bond length is as follows:
CO < CO2 < CO32-

questions.question: The pair from the following pairs having both compounds with net non-zero dipole moment is
questions.topicName: Bond Parameters
questions.explanation:
A dipole moment is the product of the magnitude of the charge and the distance between the centers of the positive and negative charges. It is denoted by the Greek letter '{tex}\mu{/tex}'.
Mathematically,
Dipole Moment {tex}(\mu)={/tex} Charge {tex}({Q}) \times{/tex} distance of separation ({tex}r{/tex})
It is measured in Debye units denoted by 'D'. 1 D {tex}=3.33564 \times 10^{-30}{/tex} C.m, where C is Coulomb and m denotes a metre.
The bond dipole moment that arises in a chemical bond between two atoms of different electronegativities can be expressed as follows:
{tex}\mu=\delta . d{/tex}
Where: {tex}\mu{/tex} is the bond dipole moment, {tex}\delta{/tex} is the magnitude of the partial charges {tex}\delta^{+}{/tex}and {tex}\delta^{-}{/tex}, And {tex}d{/tex} is the distance between {tex}\delta^{+}{/tex}and {tex}\delta^{-}{/tex}.
The bond dipole moment ({tex}\mu{/tex}) is also a vector quantity, whose direction is parallel to the bond axis. In chemistry, the arrows that are drawn in order to represent dipole moments begin at the positive charge and end at the negative charge.

questions.question:
Which of the following statement is NOT correct according to the valence bond theory?

  1. The number of unpaired electrons present in an atom is equal to the number of covalent bonds formed by the atom.
  2. Only the atomic orbitals having similar energies are involved in covalent bond formation.
  3. Geometry of the molecule is independent of the directional nature of the overlapping orbitals.
  4. Strength of the covalent bond is directly related to the extent of overlapping of atomic orbitals.
questions.topicName: Valence Bond Theory - its Important Features
questions.explanation: The geometry of the molecule is independent of the directional nature of the overlapping orbitals.

questions.question:
In which of the following molecules, overlap of p orbital is NOT involved?

  1. F2
  2. O2
  3. H2
questions.topicName: Valence Bond Theory - its Important Features
questions.explanation:
The electronic configuration of the atoms forming F2, O2 and H2 molecules are:
9F: 1s122s22p5, 8O: 1s22s22p4 and 1H: 1s1
O2 and F2 have valence electrons in the p-orbital. Hence in these molecules ‘p’ orbitals are involved in bond formation, whereas in H2 only the ‘s’ orbital is involved.

questions.question: The types of hybrid orbitals of nitrogen in {tex}{\text{NO}}_2^ + {/tex}, {tex}{\text{NO}}_3^ - {/tex} and {tex}N{\text{H}}_4^ + {/tex} respectively are expected to be
questions.topicName: Hybridization Involving s, p and d orbitals, Resonance
questions.explanation:
• In NO2+
    Number of electron pairs =2
    Number of bond pairs = 2
    Number of lone pair = 0
    So, the species is linear with sp hybridisation.
  • In {tex}NO_3^− {/tex}
    Number of electron pairs =3
    Number of bond pairs = 3
    Number of lone pair = 0
    So, the species is trigonal planar with sp​​​​2 hybridisation
  • In {tex}NH_4^+{/tex}
    Number of electron pairs =4
    Number of bond pairs = 4
    Number of lone pair = 0
    So, the species is tetrahedral with sp3 hybridisation.

questions.question: On hybridisation of one s and one p orbital we get
questions.topicName: Hybridization Involving s, p and d orbitals, Resonance
questions.explanation:
sp type of hybridization involves the intermixing of one s and one p (say Px) orbitals to give two equivalent hybrid orbitals, known as sp hybrid orbitals.
The two sp hybrid orbitals are directed in a straight line with an angle of 180° (collinear orbitals). The other two p orbitals (say py and pz) remain pure.

questions.question: AsF5 molecule is trigonal bipyramidal. The hybrid orbitals used by As atoms for bonding are:
questions.topicName: Hybridization Involving s, p and d orbitals, Resonance
questions.explanation:
The F5 has sp3d hybridization.
In sp3d hybridization, the dz2 orbital is used along with the ′s′ and three ′p′ orbitals to form three equatorial bonds and two equally strong axial bonds for a trigonal bipyramidal geometry.
Hence, the given option is correct.

questions.question: In which of the following {tex}p\pi  - d\pi {/tex} bonding is possible?
questions.topicName: Hybridization Involving s, p and d orbitals, Resonance
questions.explanation:
Carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen atoms do not have any d-orbital available. Therefore, in CO32-, NO2-, and NO3- there is no possibility of p{tex}\pi{/tex} -d{tex}\pi{/tex} type to bonding.
In PO43-, phosphorus atom has vacant d-orbital available which is used to form p{tex}\pi{/tex}-d{tex}\pi{/tex} bonding with p-orbital of the oxygen atom.

questions.question:
Among the following, the species having square planar geometry for central atom are
(i) {tex}Xe{F_4}{/tex}
(ii) {tex}S{F_4}{/tex}
(iii) {tex}{[NiC{l_4}]^{2 - }}{/tex}
(iv) {tex}{[PdC{l_4}]^{2 - }}{/tex}
questions.topicName: Hybridization Involving s, p and d orbitals, Resonance
questions.explanation:
XeF4: sp3d2  hybridization, the shape is square planar instead of octahedral due to the presence of two lone pairs of electrons on Xe atom.

SF4: SF4 molecule shows sp3d hybridization but its expected trigonal bipyramidal geometry gets distorted due to the presence of a lone pair of electrons and it becomes distorted tetrahedral or see-saw with the bond angles equal to 89∘ and177∘  instead of the expected angles of 90∘ and 180∘ respectively.


Experiments have, however, shown that 4- coordinated complexes of Pd(ll) are diamagnetic (n=0). Thus it is dsp2 hybridization which is involved in the formation of [PdCl4]2 ion i.e. 4- coordinated complexes of Pd(II) have square planar geometry with n=0 (diamagnetic).

questions.question:
Which of the following statement is true for H2S?

  1. The sulphur atom is sp3 hybridized and bonding take place in excited state.
  2. H2S and BF3 form adduct readily through coordinate bond between boron and sulphur.
  3. Its molecular geometry is non-linear and H-S-H bond angle is smaller than H-O-H bond angle in H2O.
  4. Both (ii) and (iii)
questions.topicName: Hybridization Involving s, p and d orbitals, Resonance
questions.explanation:
• The sulphur atom is sp3 hybridized and bonding takes place in the ground state rather than the excited state. Two filled orbitals 3s2 and 3px2​ are acted as lone pairs.
  • {tex}{H_2}\ddot S: \to B{F_3}{/tex}​ has a coordinate bond, S from H2​S gives lone pair and B receives it.
  • Bond angle difference leads to form hydrogen bonds in water so that two lone pairs of oxygen in water engaged in the hydrogen bond. The formation of hydrogen bonds reduces the lone pair-bond pair repulsion. That's why the bond angle is greater in the water when compared to hydrogen sulfide. In H2​S the sp3 hybrid orbitals are bigger than H2​O and so the bond pair-bond pair repulsion decreases.

questions.question: Some of the properties of the two species, {tex}\mathrm{NO}_{3}^{-}{/tex} and H3O+ are described below. Which one of them is correct?

options.option: Isostructural with same hybridization for the central atom.
Similar in hybridization for the central atom with different structures.
Isostructural with different hybridization for the central atom.
Dissimilar in hybridization for the central atom with different structures.

questions.topicName: Hybridization Involving s, p and d orbitals, Resonance
questions.explanation:
No. of electron pairs at the central atom = No. of atoms bonded to it + {tex}\frac {1}{2}{/tex}[Group number of central atom - valency of the central atom {tex}\pm{/tex} no. of electrons]
No. of electron pairs at the central atom in {tex}\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{3}}^{-}{/tex} {tex}=3+\frac{1}{2}[5-6+1]=3{/tex} (sp2 hybridisation)
No. of electron pairs at the central atom in {tex}\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}=3+\frac{1}{2}[6-3-1]=4{/tex} (sp3 hybridisation)

questions.question: In which of the following molecules the central atom does not have sp3 hybridization?
questions.topicName: Hybridization Involving s, p and d orbitals, Resonance
questions.explanation:
For neutral molecules,
No. of electron pairs = No. of atoms bonded to it + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}[Gp. no. of central atom - Valency of central atom]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} For CH4, no. of e- pairs = {tex}4+\frac{1}{2}[4-4]{/tex}
= 4(sp2 hybridisation)
For SF4, no. of pairs {tex}=4+\frac{1}{2}[6-4]{/tex}
= 5(sp3d hybridisation)
For ions,
No. of electron pairs = No. of atoms bonded to it + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}[Gp. no. of central atom - Valency of central atom {tex}\pm{/tex} No. of electrons]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} For {tex}\mathrm{BF}_{4}^{-}{/tex}, no. of e- pairs {tex}=4+\frac{1}{2}[3-4+1]{/tex}
= 4 (sp3 hybridisation)
For {tex}\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}{/tex}, no. of e- pairs {tex}=4+\frac{1}{2}[5-4-1]{/tex}
= 4 (sp3 hybridisation)

questions.question: In which one of the following species the central atom has the type of hybridization which is not the same as that present in the other three?
questions.topicName: Hybridization Involving s, p and d orbitals, Resonance
questions.explanation:
Hybridisation of the central atom can be calculated as:
{tex}H=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}[(No. of valence electrons in the central atom) + (No. of  monovalent atoms around central atom) - (Charge on cation) + (Charge on anion)]
Applying this formula we find that all the given species except [SbCl5]2- have central atom with sp3d (corresponding to H = 5) hybridization. In [SbCl5]2-, Sb is sp3d2 hybridized.

questions.explanation:
The hybridisation of the central atom can be calculated as
{tex}H=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}[(No. of valence electrons in valence shell of atom) + (No. of  monovalent atoms around central atom) - (Charge on cation) + (Charge on anion)]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} BF3, {tex}H=\frac{1}{2}[(3)+(3)-(0)+(0)]{/tex}
= 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sp2 hybridisation
For {tex}\mathrm{NO}_{2}^{-}, H=\frac{1}{2}[(5)+(0)-(0)+(1)]{/tex}
= 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sp2 hybridisation

questions.question:
Pick out the isoelectronic structures from the following:

  2. H3O+
  3. NH3
  4. {tex}\mathrm{CH}_{3}^{-}{/tex}
questions.topicName: Hybridization Involving s, p and d orbitals, Resonance
questions.explanation:
No. of e- in {tex}\mathrm{CH}_{3}^{+}{/tex} = 6 + 3 - 1 = 8
No. of e- in H3O+ = 3 + 8 - 1 = 10
No. of e- in NH3 = 7 + 3 = 10
No. of e- in CH3 = 6 + 3 + 1 = 10
{tex}\therefore{/tex} H3O+, NH3 and CH3 are isoelectronic.

{tex}\mathop {{\text{H}} - \mathop {\text{C}}\limits_{\text{*}}^{\mathop {{\text{||}}}\limits^{\text{O}} } - {\text{OH}}}\limits_{(a)}{/tex}  {tex}\mathop {{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{N}} - \mathop {\text{C}}\limits_{\text{*}}^{\mathop {\text{|}}\limits^{{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}} } = {\text{O}}}\limits_{(b)}{/tex}

(a) {tex}3 \sigma, 1 \pi{/tex}, (b) {tex}3 \sigma, 1 \pi{/tex}, (c) {tex}4 \sigma{/tex} (d) {tex}3 \sigma, 1 \pi{/tex}

questions.question: Which of the following molecules represents the order of hybridization sp2, sp2, sp, sp from left to right atoms?
questions.topicName: Hybridization Involving s, p and d orbitals, Resonance
questions.explanation:
The easiest way to determine the hybridization of a carbon, oxygen or nitrogen atom is to look at the number of {tex}\pi{/tex} bonds it forms.
If it forms no {tex}\pi{/tex} bonds, it is sp3 hybridized; if it forms one {tex}\pi{/tex} bond, it is sp2 hybridized; if it forms two {tex}\pi{/tex} bonds, it is sp hybridized.
Hybridization of C: sp(3 - no. of {tex}\pi{/tex} bonds)
{tex}\overset{{sp}^2}{{{CH}}_2} = \overset{{sp}^2}{{CH}}-\overset{{sp}}{ C}\equiv\overset{{sp}}{{CH}}{/tex}

questions.question: Decreasing order of size of various hybrid orbitals is ________.
questions.topicName: Hybridization Involving s, p and d orbitals, Resonance
questions.explanation: Percentage of s-character in hybrid orbitals is in the order sp > sp2 > sp3. Since ‘s’ orbital is closer to the nucleus, a greater ‘s’ character is associated with smaller size of the orbital. Hence sp-hybrid orbital with 50% ‘s’ character is the smallest while sp3-hybrid orbital with 25% ‘s’ character is the largest. Hence, the decreasing order of size of hybrid orbitals is sp3 > sp2 > sp.

questions.question: The correct bond order in the following species is:
questions.topicName: Molecular Orbital Theory - Its Important Features
questions.explanation:
Electronic configuration of O2+ = {tex}\sigma 1 \text s^2<\sigma^* 1 \text s^2<\sigma 2 \text s^2<{/tex} {tex}\sigma^* 2 \text s^2<\sigma 2 \text p_\text z^2<\pi 2 \text p_\text x^2{/tex} = {tex}\pi 2 \text p_\text y^2<\pi^* 2 \text p_\text x^1{/tex} = {tex}\pi^* 2 \text p_\text y^0{/tex}
Bond order = {tex}\frac{10-5}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex}
Electronic configuration of O2- = {tex}\sigma 1 s^2<\sigma^* 1 \text s^2<\sigma 2 \text s^2<{/tex} {tex}\sigma^* 2 \text s^2<\sigma 2 \text p_\text z^2<\pi 2 \text p_\text x^2 \pi{/tex} = {tex}2 \text p_\text y^2 \pi<2 \text p_\text x^2 \pi^*{/tex} = {tex}2 \text p_\text y^1 \pi^*{/tex}
Bond order = {tex}\frac{10-7}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex}
Electronic configuration of O22+ = {tex}\sigma 1 \text s^2<\sigma^* 1 \text s^2<\sigma 2 \text s^2<\sigma^* 2 \text s^2<\sigma 2 \text p_\text z^2<\pi 2 \text p_\text x^2{/tex} = {tex}\pi 2 \text p_\text y^2<\pi^* 2 \text p_\text x^0{/tex} = {tex}\pi^* 2 \text p_\text y^0{/tex}
Bond order = {tex}\frac{10-4}{2}{/tex} = 3
Thus, the correct bond order for given species is O2- < O2+ < O22+.

questions.question: Which of the followings is paramagnetic?

Electronic configuration of CO, CN- and NO+ (14 electrons)
= {tex}\sigma 1 \text s^2<\sigma^* 1 \text s^2<\sigma 2 \text s^2<{/tex} {tex}\sigma^* 2 \text s^2<\pi 2 \text p_\text x^2{/tex} = {tex}\pi 2 \text p_\text y^2<\sigma 2 \text p_\text z^2{/tex}
As no unpaired electrons. Thus, they are diamagnetic in nature.
Electronic configuration of O2- (17 electrons)
= {tex}\sigma 1 \text s^2<\sigma^* 1 \text s^2<\sigma 2 \text s^2<{/tex} {tex}\sigma^* 2 \text s^2<\sigma 2 \text p_\text z^2 <\pi 2 \text p_\text x^2{/tex} = {tex}\pi 2 \text p_\text y^2<\pi^* 2 \text p_\text x^2=\pi^* 2 \text p_\text y^1{/tex}
It contains 1 unpaired electron thus, it is paramagnetic in nature.

Electronic configuration of CO
= {tex}\sigma 1 \text s^2<\sigma^* 1 \text s^2<\sigma 2 \text s^2<{/tex} {tex}\sigma^* 2 \text s^2<\sigma 2 \text p_\text z^2 <\pi 2 \text p_\text x^2{/tex} = {tex}\pi 2 \text p_\text y^2{/tex}
It has no unpaired electron hence it is diamagnetic.
Electronic configuration of O2-
= {tex}\sigma 1 \text s^2<\sigma^* 1 \text s^2<\sigma 2 \text s^2<{/tex} {tex}\sigma^* 2 \text s^2<\sigma 2 \text p_\text z{ }^2<\pi 2 \text p_\text x{ }^2 \pi{/tex} = {tex}2 \text p_\text y^2 \pi<2 \text p_\text x^2 \pi^*<2 \text p_\text y^1 \pi^*{/tex}
It contains one unpaired electron hence, it is paramagnetic.

questions.question: The correct order of N-O bond lengths in NO, NO2-, NO3- and N2O4 is:
questions.topicName: Molecular Orbital Theory - Its Important Features
questions.explanation:
According to molecular orbital theory, higher the bond order lower will be the bond length. Now, bond order, increases as
NO3- < NO2- < N2O4 < NO thus, the bond length of N-O decreases as NO3- < NO2- > N2O4 > NO.

questions.question:
Mark the incorrect statement in the following:

  1. The bond order in the species O2, O2+ and O2- decreases as O2+ > O2 > O2-.
  2. The bond energy in a diatomic molecule always increases when an electron is lost.
  3. Electrons in antibonding MO contribute to repulsion between two atoms.
  4. With increase in bond order, bond length decrease and bond strength increases.
questions.topicName: Molecular Orbital Theory - Its Important Features
questions.explanation: It is not necessary that removal of an electron increases the bond energy. However, it depends on the orbital from which electron is removed. If electron is removed from a bonding orbital then the bond order decreases, bond length increases and bond energy decreases. If electron is removed from antibonding orbital then bond order increases, bond length decreases and energy increases.

questions.question: Which of the following is isoelectronic?
questions.topicName: Molecular Orbital Theory - Its Important Features
questions.explanation:
Number of electrons in CO2 = 6 + 2(8) = 22
Number of electrons in NO2 = 7 + 2(8) = 23
Number of electrons in NO2- = 7 + 2(8) + 1 = 24
Number of electrons in CN- = 6 + 7 + 1 = 14
Number of electrons in CO = 6 + 8 = 14
Number of electrons in SO2 = 16 + 2(8) = 32
Thus, species which are isoelectronic are CN- and CO.

questions.question: N2, CO, and NO+ are isoelectronic molecules. Their respective bond order is:
questions.topicName: Bonding In Some Homonuclear Diatomic Molecules
questions.explanation:
Total number of electrons in N2 molecule is 7+ 7= 14.
As per the formula Bonded pair of electrons Nb: {tex}σ1s^2\ σ2s^2\ {tex}\pi{/tex}2p_y^2\ {tex}\pi{/tex}2p_z^2\ σ 2p_x^2{/tex}
Total 10 electrons.
Anti bond pairs of electrons Na: {tex}σ 1s*^2\ σ 2s*^2{/tex} Total 4 electrons.
Bond Order (B.O.) = {tex}{N_b-N_a\over 2} ={ 10-4\over 2} = 3 {/tex}
Similarly bond order of CO And NO+ is 3

questions.question: Which one of the following is paramagnetic?
questions.topicName: Bonding In Some Homonuclear Diatomic Molecules
questions.explanation: The number of electrons in Nitrogen is 7 and in oxygen is 8. Hence the number of electrons that would be present in the molecular orbitals in NO is 7 + 8 = 15. As the number of electrons is odd, all the electrons in the NO molecule cannot be paired. Hence, a single electron would be present in a {tex}\pi{/tex}*2p orbital. Therefore NO is an odd electron species and the gas is hence paramagnetic due to the presence of unpaired electron.

({tex}\pi{/tex}2py2 = {tex}\pi{/tex}2px2), ({tex}\pi{/tex}*2py1 = {tex}\pi{/tex}*2px0){tex}\sigma{/tex}*2pz

questions.question: Among the following species, the diamagnetic molecule is
questions.topicName: Bonding In Some Homonuclear Diatomic Molecules
questions.explanation:
Among the given options, CO is a diamagnetic molecule. It can be proved by molecular orbital (MO) theory. The electronic configuration of given diatomic molecules are given below.

  • CO (Number of electrons = 14)
    Electronic configuration {tex} = \sigma 1{s^2},\mathop \sigma \limits^* 1{s^2},\sigma 2{s^2},\mathop \sigma \limits^* 2{s^2},\sigma 2p_z^2,\pi 2p_x^2 \approx \pi 2p_y^2{/tex}
    Since, there is no unpaired electron in the CO molecule, so it is diamagnetic.
  • No (Number of electrons = 15)
    Electronic configuration = {tex}\sigma 1{s^2},\mathop \sigma \limits^* 1{s^2},\sigma 2{s^2},\mathop \sigma \limits^* 2{s^2},\sigma 2p_z^2,\pi 2p_x^2 \approx \pi 2p_y^2,\mathop \pi \limits^* 2p_x^1 \approx \mathop \pi \limits^* 2p_y^0{/tex}
    Since, NO has one unpaired electron in {tex}\pi^{*} 2 p_{x}^{1}{/tex} orbital, so it is paramagnetic.
  • B2 (Number of electrons = 10)
    Electronic configuration {tex}\sigma {\text{l}}{s^2},\mathop \sigma \limits^* 1{s^2},\sigma 2{s^2},\mathop \sigma \limits^* 2{s^2},\pi 2p_x^1 \approx \pi p_y^1{/tex}
    Since, two unpaired electrons are present in {tex}\pi 2 p_{x}^{1}{/tex} and {tex}\pi 2 p_{y}^{1}{/tex} orbital. So, it is paramagnetic.
  • O2 (Number of electrons = 16)
    Electronic configuration {tex} = \sigma {\operatorname{ls} ^2},{\sigma ^*}1{s^2},\sigma 2{s^2},{\sigma ^*}2{s^2},\sigma 2p{z^2},\pi 2p_x^2 \approx \pi 2p_y^2,{\pi ^*}2p_x^1 \approx {\pi ^*}2p_y^1{/tex}
    Since, two unpaired electrons are present in {tex}\pi^{*} 2 p_{x}^{1}{/tex} and {tex}\pi^{*} 2 p_{y}^{1}{/tex} orbital. So, it is also paramagnetic.

questions.question: Among the following, the molecule expected to be stabilised by anion formation is C2, O2, NO, F2.
questions.topicName: Bonding In Some Homonuclear Diatomic Molecules
questions.explanation:
C2 will be stabilised after forming anion. The electronic configuration of carbon is 1s2 2s2. There are twelve electrons in C2. After forming anion (i.e. {tex}\left.\mathrm{C}_{2}^{-}\right){/tex}, the electronic configuration is

  • {tex}\mathrm{C}_{2}^{-}:(\sigma 1 s)^{2}\left(\sigma^{*} 1 s\right)^{2}(\sigma 2 s)^{2}\left(\sigma^{*} 2 s\right)^{2}{/tex}{tex}\left(\pi 2 p_{x}^{2}=\pi 2 p_{y}^{2}\right)\left(\sigma 2 p_{z}^{1}\right) \text { or } K K(\sigma 2 s)^{2}\left(\sigma^{*} 2 s\right)^{2}{/tex}{tex}\left(\pi 2 p_{x}^{2}=\pi 2 p_{y}^{2}\right) \cdot \sigma 2 p_{z}^{1}{/tex}
    Bond order {tex}=\frac{1}{2}\left(N_{b}-N_{a}\right)=\frac{1}{2}(9-4)=2.5{/tex}
    For other options such as {tex}F_2^ - ,O_2^ - ,N{O^ - },{/tex} the electronic configurations are as follows:
    {tex}\mathrm{F}_{2}^{-}:(\sigma \mathrm{l} s)^{2}\left(\sigma^{*} 1 s\right)^{2}(\sigma 2 s)^{2}\left(\sigma^{*} 2 s\right)^{2}\left(\sigma 2 p_{z}\right)^{2}{/tex}{tex}\left(\pi 2 p_{x}^{2}=\pi 2 p_{y}^{2}\right)\left(\pi^{*} 2 p_{x}^{2}=\pi^{*} 2 p_{y}^{2}\right)\left(\sigma^{*} 2 p_{z}^{1}\right){/tex}
    Bond order {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left( {{N_b} - {N_a}} \right) = \frac{1}{2}\left( {10 - 9} \right) = 0.5{/tex}
  • {tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}^{-}:(\sigma \mathrm{l} s)^{2}\left(\sigma^{*} 1 s\right)^{2}(\sigma 2 s)^{2}\left(\sigma^{*} 2 s\right)^{2}\left(\sigma 2 p_{z}\right)^{2}{/tex}{tex}\left(\pi 2 p_{x}^{2}=\pi 2 p_{y}^{2}\right)\left(\pi^{*} 2 p_{x}^{2}=\pi^{*} 2 p_{y}^{1}\right){/tex}
    Bond order {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left( {{N_b} - {N_a}} \right) = \frac{1}{2}\left( {10 - 7} \right) = 1.5{/tex}
  • {tex}N{O^ - }:{(\sigma 1s)^2}{({\sigma ^*}1s)^2}{(\sigma 2s)^2}{({\sigma ^*}2s)^2}{(\sigma 2{p_z})^2}{/tex}{tex}(\pi 2p_x^2 = \pi 2p_y^2)({\pi ^*}2p_x^1 = {\pi ^*}2p_y^1){/tex}
    Bond order {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left( {{N_b} - {N_a}} \right) = \frac{1}{2}\left( {10 - 6} \right) = 2{/tex}

The value of bond order of {tex}C_2^ - {/tex} is highest among the given options. Bond order between two atoms in a molecule may be taken as an approximate measure of the bond length.
The bond length decreases as bond order increases. As a result, the stability of a molecule increases.

questions.question: Among the following molecules/ions {tex}\mathrm{C}_{2}^{2-}, \mathrm{N}_{2}^{2-}, \mathrm{O}_{2}^{2-}, \mathrm{O}_{2}{/tex} Which one is diamagnetic and has the shortest bond length?
questions.topicName: Bonding In Some Homonuclear Diatomic Molecules
questions.explanation:
Species MO energy order Bond order (BO) n, number of unpaired e- Magnetic character
{tex}\mathrm{C}_{2}^{2-}\left(14 e^{-}\right){/tex} {tex}[8 \bar{e}] \pi_{2 p_{x}^{2}}=\pi_{2 p_{y}^{2}} \sigma_{2 p_{z}^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{6-0}{2}=3{/tex} 0 Diamagnetic
{tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}\left(16 e^{-}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{6-2}{2}=2{/tex} 2 Paramagnetic
{tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}^{2-}\left(18 e^{-}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{6-4}{2}=1{/tex} 0 Diamagnetic
{tex}\mathrm{N}_{2}^{2-}\left(16 e^{-}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{6-2}{2}=2{/tex} 2 Paramagnetic


Bond length {tex}\propto \frac{1}{B O(B o n d ~ o r d e r)}{/tex}. So order of bond length {tex}\mathop {C_2^{2 - }}\limits_{(BO = 3)} < \mathop {{O_2}}\limits_{(BO = 2)} = N_2^{2 - } < \mathop {O_2^{2 - }}\limits_{(BO = 1)} {/tex}
The diamagnetic spoecies with shortest bond length is {tex}\mathrm{C}_{2}^{2-}{/tex}

questions.question: Two pi and half sigma bonds are present in
questions.topicName: Bonding In Some Homonuclear Diatomic Molecules
questions.explanation:
The energy order of MOs of the given species are as follows:
{tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}\left(16 e^{-\prime} \mathrm{s}\right)=\sigma \mathrm{l} s^{2}, \sigma^{*} \mathrm{l} s^{2}, \sigma 2 s^{2}, \sigma^{*} 2 s^{2}, \sigma 2 p_{z}^{2}{/tex},
{tex}\pi 2 p_{x}^{2}=\pi 2 p_{y}^{2}, \pi^{*} 2 p_{x}^{1}=\pi^{*} 2 p_{y}^{1}{/tex},
{tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}^{+}\left(15 e^{-\prime} \mathrm{s}\right)=\sigma \mathrm{ls}^{2}, \sigma^{*} 1 s^{2}, \sigma 2 s^{2}, \sigma^{*} 2 s^{2}, \sigma 2 p_{z}^{2}{/tex},
{tex}\pi 2 p_{x}^{2}=\pi 2 p_{y}^{2}, \pi^{*} 2 p_{x}^{1} \approx \pi^{*} 2 p_{y}^{0}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{N}_{2}\left(14 e^{-\prime} \mathrm{s}\right)=\sigma 1 s^{2} \sigma^{*} 1 s^{2}, \sigma 2 s^{2}, \sigma^{*} 2 s^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\pi 2 p_{x}^{2}=\pi 2 p_{y}^{2}, \sigma 2 p_{z}^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{N}_{2}^{+}\left(13 e^{-\prime} \mathrm{s}\right)=\sigma \mathrm{ls}^{2} \sigma^{*} 1 s^{2} \sigma 2 s^{2} \sigma^{*} 2 s^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\pi 2 p_{x}^{2}=\pi 2 p_{z}^{2} \sigma 2 p_{z}^{1}{/tex}
Thus, in case of {tex}\mathrm{N}_{2}^{+}{/tex}, two {tex}\pi{/tex}-bonds and half {tex}\sigma {/tex}-bond are present in the bonding MOs.

questions.question: According to molecular orbital theory, which of the following is true with respect to {tex}\mathrm{Li}_{2}^{+}{/tex} and {tex}\mathrm{Li}_{2}^{-}{/tex}?
questions.topicName: Bonding In Some Homonuclear Diatomic Molecules
questions.explanation:
Considering molecular orbital theory (MOT):
The electronic configuration of {tex}\mathrm{Li}_{2}^{+}(Z=5)=\sigma \mathrm{ls}^{2}, \sigma^{*} \mathrm{Is}^{2}, \sigma 2 s^{1}{/tex}
Bond order (BO) {tex}=\frac{N_{b}-N_{a}}{2}=\frac{3-2}{2}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
The electronic configuration of {tex}\mathrm{Li}_{2}^{-}(Z=7)=\sigma \mathrm{ls}^{2}, \sigma^{*} 1 s^{2}, \sigma 2 s^{2}, \sigma^{*} 2 s^{1}{/tex}
For the species having the same value of BO, the specie having lesser number of antibonding electrons [Na] will be more stable.
Here, Na of {tex}\mathrm{Li}_{2}^{+}(2)<N_{a}{/tex} of {tex}Li_2^ - {/tex} (3). So, their order of stability will be {tex}\mathrm{Li}_{2}^{+}>\mathrm{Li}_{2}^{-}{/tex}.

questions.question: According to molecular orbital theory, which of the following will not be a viable molecule?
questions.topicName: Bonding In Some Homonuclear Diatomic Molecules
questions.explanation:
Electronic Configuration Bond order
{tex}\mathrm{He}_{2}^{+}{/tex} {tex}\sigma_{1 s^{2}} \sigma^{*}_{1 s^1}{/tex} {tex}\frac{2-1}{2}=0.5{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{H}_{2}^{-}{/tex} {tex}\sigma_{1 s^{2}} \sigma^{*}_{1 s^1}{/tex} {tex}\frac{2-1}{2}=0.5{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{H}_{2}^{2-}{/tex} {tex}\sigma_{1 s^{2}} \sigma^{*}_{1 s^2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{2-2}{2}=0{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{He}_{2}^{2+}{/tex} {tex}{\sigma _{1{s^2}}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{2-0}{2}=1{/tex}

The molecule having zero bond order will not be viable hence, {tex}\mathrm{H}_{2}^{2-}{/tex} is the correct answer.

questions.question: Which of the following species is not paramagnetic?
questions.topicName: Bonding In Some Homonuclear Diatomic Molecules
questions.explanation:
To identify the magnetic nature we need to check the molecular orbital configuration. If all orbitals are fully occupied, species is diamagnetic while when one or more molecular orbitals is/are singly occupied, species is paramagnetic.

  1. {tex}\mathrm{NO}(7+8=15)-\sigma \operatorname{ls}^{2}, \sigma^{*} 1 s^{2}, \sigma 2 s^{2}, \sigma^{*} 2 s^{2}{/tex}
    {tex}\pi 2 p_{x}^{2}=\pi 2 p_{y}^{2}, \pi 2 p_{z}^{2}, \pi^{*} 2 p_{x}^{1}=\pi^{*} 2 p_{y}^{0}{/tex}
    One unpaired electron is present. Hence, it is paramagnetic.
  2. {tex}\mathrm{CO}(6+8=14)-\sigma \mathrm{ls}^{2}, \sigma^{*} 1 s^{2}, \sigma 2 s^{2}, \sigma^{*} 2 s^{2}{/tex}
    {tex}\pi 2 p_{x}^{2}=\pi 2 p_{y}^{2}, \sigma 2 p_{z}^{2}{/tex}
    No unpaired electron is present. Hence, it is diamagnetic.
  3. {tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}(8+8=16)-\sigma \mathrm{ls}^{2}, \sigma^{*} 1 s^{2}, \sigma 2 s^{2}, \sigma^{*} 2 s^{2}, \sigma 2 p_{z}^{2}{/tex}
    {tex}\pi 2 p_{x}^{2}=\pi 2 p_{x}^{2}, \pi^{*} 2 p_{x}^{1}=\pi^{*} 2 p_{y}^{1}{/tex}
    Two unpaired electrons are present.
    Hence, it is paramagnetic.
  4. {tex}\mathrm{B}_{2}(5+5)-\sigma \mathrm{ls}^{2}, \sigma^{*} \mathrm{ls}^{2}, \sigma 2 s^{2}, \sigma^{*} 2 s^{2}, \pi 2 p_{x}^{1}=\pi 2 p_{y}^{1}{/tex}
    Two unpaired electrons are present.
    Hence, it is paramagnetic.

questions.question: Assuming 2s-2p mixing is NOT operative, the paramagnetic species among the following is
questions.topicName: Bonding In Some Homonuclear Diatomic Molecules
questions.explanation:
Assuming that no 2s-2p mixing takes place the molecular orbital electronic configuration can be written in the following sequence of energy levels of molecular orbitals
{tex}\sigma \mathrm{l} s, \stackrel{*}{\sigma} \mathrm{ls}, \sigma 2 s, \stackrel{*}{\sigma} 2 s, \sigma 2 p_{z}, \pi 2 p_{x}=\pi 2 p_{y}, \quad \pi 2 p_{x} \equiv \pi 2 p_{y}, \stackrel{*}{\sigma} 2 p_{z}{/tex}

  1. {tex}{\text{B}}{{\text{e}}_2} \to \sigma {\text{l}}{{\text{s}}^2},\mathop \sigma \limits^* {\text{l}}{{\text{s}}^2},\sigma 2{s^2},\mathop \sigma \limits^* 2{s^2}({\text{ diamagnetic }}){/tex}
  2. {tex}\mathrm{B}_{2} \rightarrow \sigma \mathrm{ls}^{2}, \vec{\sigma} 1 s^{2}, \sigma 2 s^{2}, \stackrel{*}{\sigma} 2 s^{2}, \sigma 2 p_{z}^{2}, \begin{array}{l} {\pi 2 p_{x}^{0}} \\ {\pi 2 p_{x}^{0}} \end{array} \text { (diamagnetic) }{/tex}
  3. {tex}\mathrm{C}_{2} \rightarrow \sigma \mathrm{l} s^{2}, \stackrel{*}{\sigma} 1 s^{2}, \sigma 2 s^{2}, \stackrel{*}{\sigma} 2 s^{2}, \sigma 2 p_{z}^{2}, \begin{array}{c} {\pi 2 p_{x}^{\prime}} \\ {\pi 2 p_{y}^{\prime}} \end{array}, _{\mathop \pi \limits^* 2p_y^0}^{\mathop \pi \limits^* 2p_x^0},\mathop \sigma \limits^* 2p_z^0(paramagnetic){/tex}
  4. {tex}{{\text{N}}_2} \to \sigma {\text{l}}{s^2},\mathop \sigma \limits^* {\operatorname{ls} ^2},\sigma 2{s^2},\mathop \sigma \limits^* 2{s^2},\sigma 2p_z^2,\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {\pi 2p_x^2} \\ {\pi 2p_y^2} \end{array},{/tex}{tex}\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {\mathop \pi \limits^* 2p_x^0} \\ {\mathop \pi \limits^* 2p_y^0} \end{array},{\sigma ^*}2p_z^0{/tex} (diamagnetic)

Hence, C2 is the correct choice.

questions.question: Stability of the species Li2, {tex}\mathrm{Li}_{2}^{-}{/tex} and {tex}\mathrm{Li}_{2}^{+}{/tex} increases in the order of
questions.topicName: Bonding In Some Homonuclear Diatomic Molecules
questions.explanation:
{tex}{\text{L}}{{\text{i}}_2}(3 + 3 = 6) = \sigma {\text{l}}{{\text{s}}^2},\;\mathop \sigma \limits^* {\text{l}}{{\text{s}}^2},\sigma 2{{\text{s}}^2}{/tex}
Bond order {tex}=\frac{N_{b}-N_{a}}{2}=\frac{4-2}{2}=1{/tex}
{tex}{\text{Li}}_2^ + (3 + 3 - 1 = 5) = \sigma {\text{l}}{s^2},\mathop \sigma \limits^* 1{s^2},\sigma 2{s^1}{/tex}
Bond order {tex}=\frac{3-2}{2}=\frac{1}{2}=0.5{/tex}
{tex}{\text{Li}}_2^ - (3 + 3 + 1 = 7) = \sigma {\text{l}}{{\text{s}}^2},\;\mathop \sigma \limits^* 1{s^2},\sigma 2{s^2}\;\mathop \sigma \limits^* 2{s^1}{/tex}
Bond order {tex}=\frac{4-3}{2}=\frac{1}{2}=0.5{/tex}
Stability order is {tex}\mathrm{Li}_{2}<\mathrm{Li}_{2}^{+}<\mathrm{Li}_{2}{/tex} (because {tex}{\text{Li}}_2^ - {/tex} has more number of electrons in antibonding orbitals which destabilises the species).

questions.question: In which of the following pairs of molecules/ions both the species are not likely to exist?
questions.topicName: Bonding In Some Homonuclear Diatomic Molecules
questions.explanation:
Species having zero or negative bond order do not exist.
{tex}\mathrm{H}_{2}^{2+}(1+1-2=0)=\sigma \mathrm{l} s^{0}{/tex}
Bond order = 0
{tex}\mathrm{He}_{2}(2+2=4)=\sigma \mathrm{l} s^{2}, \stackrel{*}{\sigma} \mathrm{ls}^{2}{/tex}
Bond order {tex}=\frac{N_{b}-N_{a}}{2}=\frac{2-2}{2}=0{/tex}
So, both {tex}\mathrm{H}_{2}^{2+}{/tex} and He2 do not exist.

{tex}{\text{O}}_2^ + \left( {15{e^ - }} \right):\sigma 1{s^2}\mathop \sigma \limits^* 1{s^2}\sigma 2{s^2}\mathop \sigma \limits^* 2{s^2}\sigma 2p_x^2{/tex}{tex}\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\pi 2p_y^2} \\ {\pi 2p_z^2} \end{array}} \right|\left. {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\mathop \pi \limits^* 2p_y^1} \\ {\mathop \pi \limits^* 2p_z^0} \end{array}} \right|\mathop \sigma \limits^* 2p_x^0{/tex}
Bond order {tex}=\frac{10-5}{2}=2.5;{/tex} paramagnetic.

Bond order {tex}=\frac{10-6}{2}=2{/tex}
Hence, ({tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}^{+}{/tex} is paramagnetic and bond order greater than O2) is the correct answer.

questions.question: Which of the following option w.r.t. increasing bond order is correct?
questions.topicName: Bonding In Some Homonuclear Diatomic Molecules
questions.explanation:
The bond order of the molecules:
{tex}O_2^-: KK(\sigma2s)^2 (\sigma^*2s)^2(\sigma2p_z)^2 (\pi2p_x)^2 (\pi2p_y)^2 (\pi^*2p_x)^2 (\pi^*2p_y)^1{/tex}
{tex}The \ Bond \ order \ of \ O_2^- = \frac{1}{2}(8-5) = 1.5{/tex}
{tex}He_2^+: (\sigma1s)^2 (\sigma^*1s)^1{/tex}
{tex}The \ Bond \ order \ of \ He_2^+ = \frac{1}{2}(2-1) = 0.5{/tex}
{tex}C_2: KK(\sigma2s)^2 (\sigma^*2s)^2(\pi2p_x)^2 (\pi2p_y)^2 {/tex}
{tex}The \ Bond \ order \ of \ C = \frac{1}{2}(6-2) = 2.0{/tex}
{tex}NO_: KK(\sigma2s)^2 (\sigma^*2s)^2(\sigma2p_z)^2 (\pi2p_x)^2 (\pi2p_y)^2 (\pi^*2p_x)^1{/tex}
{tex}The \ Bond \ order \ of \ NO = \frac{1}{2}(8-3) = 2.5{/tex}  
Hence, the correct order is {tex}H{e^ + } < {{\text{O}}_2}^ -  < {C_2} < NO{/tex}.

questions.question: In which of the following process, the increase in bond length is highest?

questions.explanation:
1. {tex}\mathrm{N}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{N}_{2}^{+}{/tex} (B.L. {tex}\uparrow{/tex}, Bonding e- {tex}\downarrow{/tex})
  2. {tex}\mathrm{N}_{2}^{+} \rightarrow \mathrm{N}_{2}^{-}{/tex} (B.L. {tex}\uparrow{/tex}, antibonding e- {tex}\uparrow{/tex})
  3. {tex}\mathrm{N}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{N}_{2}^{-}{/tex} (B.L. {tex}\uparrow{/tex})
  4. {tex}\mathrm{N}_{2}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{N}_{2}^{+}{/tex} (B. L. {tex}\downarrow{/tex})

So, the correct order of B.L. is {tex}\mathrm{N}_{2}>\mathrm{N}_{2}^{+}>\mathrm{N}_{2}^{-}{/tex}.

questions.question:
The ground state electronic configuration of valence shell electrons in nitrogen molecule (N2) is written as {tex}K K, \sigma 2 s^{2}, \sigma^{*} 2 s^{2}, \pi 2 p_{x}^{2}=\pi 2 p_{y}^{2}, \sigma 2 p_{z}^{2}{/tex}.
Hence, the bond order in nitrogen molecule is:
questions.topicName: Bonding In Some Homonuclear Diatomic Molecules
questions.explanation:
Number of electrons in bonding orbitals Nb = 10 and number of electrons in anti bonding orbitals Na = 4.
Therefore bond order = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}(Nb - Na)
= {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}(10 - 4) = 3

questions.question:
Which of the following species is paramagnetic?

  1. CO
  2. CN-
  3. {tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}^{2-}{/tex} 
  4. NO
questions.topicName: Bonding In Some Homonuclear Diatomic Molecules
questions.explanation: Paramagnetism is caused by the presence of atoms, ions or molecules with unpaired electrons, i.e. {tex}: \dot{\mathrm{N}}=\ddot{\mathrm{O:}}{/tex}

questions.question: N2 and O2 are converted into monoanions {tex}\mathrm{N}_{2}^{-}{/tex} and {tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}^{-}{/tex} respectively, which of the following statements is wrong?

options.option: In N_2^-, the N-N bond weakens

questions.topicName: Bonding In Some Homonuclear Diatomic Molecules
questions.explanation:
Electronic configuration of N2 = {tex}\sigma 1 \text s^2<\sigma^* 1 \text s^2<\sigma 2 \text s^2<{/tex} {tex}\sigma^* 2 \text s^2<\pi 2 \text p_\text x^2{/tex} = {tex}\pi 2 \text p_\text y^2<\sigma 2 \text p_\text z^2{/tex}
Electronic configuration of O2 = {tex}\sigma 1 \text s^2<\sigma^* 1 \text s^2<\sigma 2 \text s^2<{/tex} {tex}\sigma^* 2 \text s^2<\sigma 2 \text p_\text z^2<\pi 2 \text p_\text x^2{/tex} = {tex}\pi 2 \text p_\text y^2<\pi^* 2 \text p_\text x^1<\pi^* 2 \text p_\text y^1{/tex}
When N2- is formed then one extra electron is added to anti bonding orbital thus, the bond weakens as one single unpaired electron is added to anti bonding orbitals thus, it is paramagnetic and not diamagnetic.
When O2- is formed then one extra electron is added to anti bonding orbital thus, bond order decreases and bond length increases.

questions.question:
N2 and O2 are converted into monocations, {tex}\mathrm{N}_{2}^{+}{/tex} and {tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}^{+}{/tex} respectively. Which is wrong?

  1. In O2 paramagnetism decreases.
  2. {tex}\mathrm{N}_{2}^{+}{/tex} becomes diamagnetic.
  3. In N2, the N—N bond weakens.
  4. In O2, the O—O bond order increases.
questions.topicName: Bonding In Some Homonuclear Diatomic Molecules
questions.explanation: Diamagnetism is caused due to the absence of unpaired electrons. But in N2+, there is unpaired electron. So, it is paramagnetic.

questions.question: The number of anti-bonding electron pairs in {tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}^{2-}{/tex} molecular ion on the basis of molecular orbital theory is (Atomic number of O is 8):
questions.topicName: Bonding In Some Homonuclear Diatomic Molecules
questions.explanation:
{tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}^{2-}(18) \rightarrow(\sigma 1 s)^{2},\left(\sigma^{*} 1 s\right)^{2}(\sigma 2 s)^{2},\left(\sigma^{*} 2 s\right)^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\left(\sigma 2 p_{x}\right)^{2},\left(\pi 2 p_{y}\right)^{2},\left(\pi 2 p_{z}\right)^{2},\left(\pi^{*} 2 p_{y}\right)^{2},\left(\pi^{*} 2 p_{z}\right)^{2} \rightarrow{/tex}
represents antibonding molecular orbitals. Thus the no. of antibonding electrons in {tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}^{2-}{/tex} ion is = 8(4 pairs)

questions.question:
Which species does not exhibit paramagnetism?

  1. {tex}\mathrm{N}_{2}^{+}{/tex} 
  2. {tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}^{-}{/tex} 
  3. CO
  4. NO
questions.topicName: Bonding In Some Homonuclear Diatomic Molecules
questions.explanation: In 'CO' (14 electrons), there is no unpaired electron in its molecular orbital. Therefore, this does not exhibit paramagnetism.

questions.question: The relationship between the dissociation energy of N2 and N2+ is:
questions.topicName: Bonding In Some Homonuclear Diatomic Molecules
questions.explanation:
{tex}\mathrm{N}_{2}(14) \rightarrow(\sigma 1 s)^{2},\left(\sigma^{*} 1 s\right)^{2},(\sigma 2 s)^{2}{/tex},
{tex}\left(\sigma^{*} 2 s\right)^{2},\left(\pi 2 p_{x}\right)^{2},\left(\pi 2 p_{y}\right)^{2},\left(\sigma 2 p_{z}\right)^{2}{/tex}
In N2, bond order {tex}=\frac{N_{b}-N_{a}}{2}=\frac{10-4}{2}=3{/tex}
In {tex}\mathrm{N}_{2}^{+}{/tex} bond order {tex}=\frac{9-4}{2}{/tex} = 2.5
As the bond order in N2 is more than {tex}\mathrm{N}_{2}^{+}{/tex} so the dissociation energy of N2 is higher than {tex}\mathrm{N}_{2}^{+}{/tex}.

questions.question: Four diatomic species are listed below in different sequences. Which of these presents the correct order of their increasing bond order?
questions.topicName: Bonding In Some Homonuclear Diatomic Molecules
questions.explanation:
According to molecular orbital theory, the energy level of the given molecules are:
{tex}\mathrm{C}_{2}^{2-} \rightarrow \sigma 1 s^{2}, \sigma^{*} 1 s^{2} \sigma 2 s^{2} \sigma^{*} 2 s^{2} \pi 2 p_{x}^{2}, \pi 2 p_{y}^{2}, \sigma 2 p_{z}^{2}{/tex}
B.O. = 1/2[10 - 4] = 3
{tex}\mathrm{He}_{2}^{+} \rightarrow \sigma 1 s^{2} \sigma^{*} 1 s^{1}{/tex}
B.O. = 1/2[2 -1] = 1/2 = 0.5
{tex}\mathrm{NO} \rightarrow \sigma 1 s^{2} \sigma^{*} 1 s^{2} \sigma 2 s^{2} \sigma^{*} 2 s^{2} \pi 2 p_{x}^{2} \approx \pi 2 p_{y}^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\sigma 2 p_{2}^{2} \pi^{*} 2 p_{x}^{1}{/tex}
B.O. - 1/2[10 - 5] = 2.5
{tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}^{-} \rightarrow \sigma 1 s^{2} \sigma^{*} 1 s^{2} \sigma 2 s^{2} \sigma^{*} 2 s^{2} \sigma 2 p_{z}^{2} \pi 2 p_{x}^{2} \pi 2 p_{y}^{2}{/tex} {tex}\pi^{*} 2 p_{x}^{2} \pi^{*} 2 p_{y}^{1}{/tex}
B.O. = 1/2[10 - 7] = 1.5
So, the correct order of their increasing bond order is {tex}\mathrm{He}_{2}^{+}<\mathrm{O}_{2}^{-}<\mathrm{NO}<\mathrm{C}_{2}^{2-}{/tex}

questions.question: According to molecular orbital theory which of the lists ranks the nitrogen species in terms of increasing bond order?
questions.topicName: Bonding In Some Homonuclear Diatomic Molecules
questions.explanation:
According to MOT, the molecular orbital electronic configuration of
{tex}\mathrm{N}_{2}:(\sigma 1 s)^{2}\left(\sigma^{*} 1 s\right)^{2}(\sigma 2 s)^{2}\left(\sigma^{*} 2 s\right)^{2}\left(\pi 2 p_{x}\right)^{2}{/tex} {tex}\left(\pi 2 p_{y}\right)^{2}\left(\sigma 2 p_{z}\right)^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \text { B.O. }=\frac{10-4}{2}=3{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{N}_{2}^{-}:(\sigma 1 s)^{2}\left(\sigma^{*} 1 s\right)^{2}(\sigma 2 s)^{2}\left(\sigma^{*} 2 s\right)^{2}\left(\pi 2 p_{x}\right)^{2}\left(\pi^{*} p_{y}\right)^{2}{/tex} {tex}\left(\sigma 2 p_{z}\right)^{2}\left(\pi^{*} 2 p_{x}\right)^{1}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \text { B.O. }=\frac{10-5}{2}=2.5{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{N}_{2}^{2-}:(\sigma 1 s)^{2}\left(\sigma^{*} 1 s\right)^{2}(\sigma 2 s)^{2}\left(\sigma^{*} 2 s\right)^{2}\left(\pi 2 p_{x}\right)^{2}\left(\pi 2 p_{y}\right)^{2}{/tex} {tex}\left(\sigma 2 p_{z}\right)^{2}\left(\pi^{*} 2 p_{x}\right)^{1}\left(\pi^{*} 2 p_{y}\right)^{1}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \text { B.O. }=\frac{10-6}{2}=2{/tex}
Hence, the order: {tex}\mathrm{N}_{2}^{2-}<\mathrm{N}_{2}^{-}<\mathrm{N}_{2}{/tex}.

questions.question: Which one of the following species does not exist under normal conditions?
questions.topicName: Bonding In Some Homonuclear Diatomic Molecules
questions.explanation:
Be2 does not exist.
Be2 has an electronic configuration of:
{tex}\sigma 1 s^{2} \sigma^{*} 1 s^{2} \sigma 2 s^{2} \sigma^{*} 2 s^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Bond order {tex}=\frac{4-4}{2}{/tex} = 0
Thus, Be2 does not exist.

questions.question: The pairs of species of oxygen and their magnetic behaviour are noted below. Which of the following presents the correct description?
questions.topicName: Bonding In Some Homonuclear Diatomic Molecules
questions.explanation: {tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}^{+}{/tex} and O2 are paramagnetic in nature as they contain one and two unpaired electrons respectively.

questions.question:
Which of the following has the minimum bond length?

  1. {tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}^{+}{/tex}
  2. {tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}^{-}{/tex} 
  3. {tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}^{2-}{/tex} 
  4. O2
questions.topicName: Bonding In Some Homonuclear Diatomic Molecules
questions.explanation:
Electronic configuration
{tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}:(\sigma 2 s)^{2}\left(\sigma^{*} 2 s\right)^{2}\left(\sigma 2 p_{x}\right)^{2}\left(\pi 2 p_{y}\right)^{2}\left(\pi 2 p_{z}\right)^{2}{/tex} {tex}\left(\pi^{*} 2 p_{y}\right)^{1}\left(\pi^{*} 2 p_{z}\right)^{1}{/tex}
Bond order = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (8 - 4) = 2
{tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}^{+}{/tex} : Bond order = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}(8 - 3) = {tex}2 \frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}^{-}{/tex} : Bond order = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}(8 - 5) = {tex}1 \frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}^{2-}{/tex} : Bond order = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (8 - 6) = 1
As bond order increases, bond length decreases.

questions.question: During change of O2 to {tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}^{-}{/tex} ion, the electron adds on which one of the following orbitals?
questions.topicName: Bonding In Some Homonuclear Diatomic Molecules
questions.explanation:
Electronic configuration of O
{tex}\sigma(1 s)^{2} \sigma^{*}(1 s)^{2} \sigma(2 s)^{2} \sigma^{*}(2 s)^{2} \sigma\left(2 p_{z}\right)^{2} \pi\left(2 p_{x}\right)^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\pi\left(2 p_{y}\right)^{2} \pi^{*}\left(2 p_{x}\right)^{1} \pi^{*}\left(2 p_{y}\right)^{1}{/tex}
Thus the incoming electron will enter in {tex}\pi^{*} 2 p_{x}{/tex} to form {tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}^{-}{/tex}.

questions.question:
The pair of species with the same bond order is:

  1. {tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}^{2-}{/tex}, B2 
  2. {tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}^{+}{/tex}, NO+ 
  3. NO, CO
  4. N2, O2
questions.topicName: Bonding In Some Homonuclear Diatomic Molecules
questions.explanation:
{tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}^{2-} \rightarrow 1{/tex}; B2 {tex}\to{/tex} 1
{tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}^{+} \rightarrow 2.5{/tex}; NO+ {tex}\to{/tex} 3
NO {tex}\to{/tex} 2.5; CO {tex}\to{/tex} 3
N2 {tex}\to{/tex} 3; O2 {tex}\to{/tex} 2.0

questions.question: Bond order of 1.5 is shown by:
questions.topicName: Bonding In Some Homonuclear Diatomic Molecules
questions.explanation:
Configuration of O2
{tex}\sigma 1 s^{2} \sigma^{*} 1 s^{2} \sigma 2 s^{2} \sigma^{*} 2 s^{2} \sigma 2 p_{z}^{2} \pi 2 p_{x}^{2} \pi 2 p_{y}^{2} \pi^{*} 2 p_{x}^{1} \pi^{*} 2 p_{y}^{1}{/tex}
Bond order {tex}=\frac{No.\;of\;e^-\;in\;bonding\;M.O.-No.\;of\;e^-\;in\;antibonding\;M.O.}2{/tex}
Bond order of {tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}^{+}=\frac{10-5}{2}=2.5{/tex}
Bond order of {tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}^{-}=\frac{10-7}{2}=1.5{/tex}
Bond order of {tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}^{2-}=\frac{10-8}{2}=1.0{/tex}
Bond order of O2 {tex}=\frac{10-6}{2}=2{/tex}

questions.question:
Which of the following is paramagnetic?

  1. CN-
  2. NO+
  3. CO
  4. {tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}^{-}{/tex}
questions.topicName: Bonding In Some Homonuclear Diatomic Molecules
questions.explanation:
{tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}^{-}{/tex}(17) superoxide has one unpaired electron.
{tex}\sigma 1 s^{2} \sigma^{*} 1 s^{2} \sigma 2 s^{2} \sigma^{*} 2 s^{2} \sigma 2 p_{z}^{2} \pi 2 p_{x}^{2}=\pi 2 p_{y}^{2} \pi^{*} 2 p_{x}^{2} =\pi^{*} 2 p_{y}^{1}{/tex}

questions.question: In which of the following ionization processes the bond energy increases and the magnetic behaviour changes from paramagnetic to diamagnetic?
questions.topicName: Bonding In Some Homonuclear Diatomic Molecules
questions.explanation:
Molecular orbital configuration of
{tex}\mathrm{O}_{2} \Rightarrow \sigma 1 s^{2} \sigma^{*} 1 s^{2} \sigma 2 s^{2} \sigma^{*} 2 s^{2} \sigma 2 p_{z}^{2} \pi 2 p_{x}^{2} \pi 2 p_{y}^{2} \pi^{*} 2 p_{x}^{1} \pi^{*} 2 p_{y}^{1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Paramagnetic
Bond order {tex}=\frac{10-6}{2}=2{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}^{+} \Rightarrow \sigma 1 s^{2} \sigma^{*} 1 s^{2} \sigma 2 s^{2} \sigma^{*} 2 s^{2} \sigma 2 p_{z}^{2} \pi 2 p_{x}^{2} \pi 2 p_{y}^{2} \pi^{*} 2 p_{x}^{1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Paramagnetic
Bond order {tex}=\frac{10-5}{2}=2.5{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{C}_{2} \Rightarrow \sigma 1 s^{2} \sigma^{*} 1 s^{2} \sigma 2 s^{2} \sigma^{*} 2 s^{2} \pi 2 p_{x}^{2} \pi 2 p_{y}^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Diamagnetic
Bond order {tex}=\frac{8-4}{2}=2{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{C}_{2}^{+} \Rightarrow \sigma 1 s^{2} \sigma^{*} 1 s^{2} \sigma 2 s^{2} \sigma^{*} 2 s^{2} \pi 2 p_{x}^{2} \pi 2 p_{y}^{1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Paramagnetic
Bond order {tex}=\frac{7-4}{2}=1.5{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{NO} \Rightarrow \sigma 1 s^{2} \sigma^{*} 1 s^{2} \sigma 2 s^{2} \sigma^{*} 2 s^{2} \sigma 2 p_{z}^{2} \pi 2 p_{x}^{2} \pi 2 p_{y}^{2} \pi^{*} 2 p_{x}^{1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Paramagnetic
Bond order {tex}=\frac{10-5}{2}=2.5{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{NO}^{+} \Rightarrow \sigma 1 s^{2} \sigma^{*} 1 s^{2} \sigma 2 s^{2} \sigma^{*} 2 s^{2} \sigma 2 p_{z}^{2} \pi 2 p_{x}^{2} \pi 2 p_{y}^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Diamagnetic
Bond order {tex}=\frac{10-4}{2}=3{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{N}_{2} \Rightarrow \sigma 1 s^{2} \sigma^{*} 1 s^{2} \sigma 2 s^{2} \sigma^{*} 2 s^{2} \pi 2 p_{x}^{2} \pi 2 p_{y}^{2} \sigma 2 p_{z}^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Paramagnetic
Bond order {tex}=\frac{10-4}{2}=3{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{N}_{2}^{+} \Rightarrow \sigma 1 s^{2} \sigma^{*} 1 s^{2} \sigma 2 s^{2} \sigma^{*} 2 s^{2} \pi 2 p_{x}^{2} \pi 2 p_{y}^{2} \sigma 2 p_{z}^{1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Paramagnetic
Bond order {tex}=\frac{9-4}{2}=2.5{/tex}

questions.question:

questions.topicName: Bonding In Some Homonuclear Diatomic Molecules
