questions.question: Decreasing order of stability of O2, {tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}^{-}, \mathrm{O}_{2}^{+}{/tex} and {tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}^{2-}{/tex} is:
questions.topicName: Bonding In Some Homonuclear Diatomic Molecules
questions.explanation:
{tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}(16): K K \sigma 2 s^{2} \sigma^{*} 2 s^{2} \sigma 2 p_{z}^{2} \pi 2 p_{x}^{2}=\pi 2 p_{y}^{2} \pi^{*} 2 p_{x}^{1}=\pi^{*} 2 p_{y}^{1}{/tex}
Bond order = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}(8 - 4) = 2
{tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}^{2-}(18): K K \sigma 2 s^{2} \sigma^{*} 2 s^{2} \sigma 2 p_{z}^{2} \pi 2 p_{x}^{2}=\pi 2 p_{y}^{2} \pi^{*} 2 p_{x}^{2}=\pi^{*} 2 p_{y}^{2}{/tex}
Bond order = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}(8 - 6) = 1
{tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}^{-}(17): K K \sigma 2 s^{2} \sigma^{*} 2 s^{2} \sigma 2 p_{z}^{2} \pi 2 p_{x}^{2}=\pi 2 p_{y}^{2} \pi^{*} 2 p_{x}^{2}=\pi^{*} 2 p_{y}^{1}{/tex}
Bond order = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}(8 - 5) = 1.5
{tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}^{+}(15): K K \sigma 2 s^{2} \sigma^{*} 2 s^{2} \sigma 2 p_{z}^{2} \pi 2 p_{x}^{2}=\pi 2 p_{y}^{2} \pi^{*} 2 p_{x}^{1}{/tex}
Bond order = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}(8 - 3) = 2.5
As, bond order {tex}\propto{/tex} stability
The decreasing order of stability is {tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}^{+}>\mathrm{O}_{2}>\mathrm{O}_{2}^{-}>\mathrm{O}_{2}^{2-}{/tex}

questions.question:

Electronic configuration of He atom = 1s2
According to the Linear combination of atomic orbitals, the He 2 ​ molecule electronic configuration is - {tex}\sigma{/tex}1s2{tex}\sigma{/tex}*1s2
Thus there are equal numbers of electrons present in bonding molecular orbitals and anti-bonding molecular orbitals.
He2 = Total electron = 4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} B.O. = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} [Nb - Na] = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} [2 - 2] = 0
Bond order in He2 molecule = 0

Thus there is no existence of the He2 (diatomic Helium) molecule.

  1. Bond order for both is one
  2. OF is paramagnetic but F2 is diamagnetic
  3. F2 is more likely to dissociate into atoms than OF
  4. Both have greater number of electrons in BMO than that in ABMO

Select the correct set of codes using T for the statement and F for the incorrect statement in the given sequence.
questions.topicName: Bonding In Some Homonuclear Diatomic Molecules
questions.explanation:
B.O. of F2 = 1 (diamagnetic)
B.O. of OF = 1.5 (paramagnetic)

questions.explanation:
O3 (ozone) is diamagnetic.
Maximum bond order is in {tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}^{+}{/tex}
Maximum unpaired e- is in O2 (Two).

questions.question: Which of the following molecule doesn’t exist?
questions.topicName: Bonding In Some Homonuclear Diatomic Molecules
questions.explanation:
H2
The electronic configuration of He2 is σ1s2σ*1s2. Here NA = NB (where NA as the number of anti-bonding molecular orbitals and NB as the number of bonding molecular orbitals). So the molecule He2 doesn’t exist.

{tex}\begin{array}{c} \mathrm{N}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{N}_{2}^{+} \\ (\mathrm{B} . \mathrm{O}=3) \quad(\mathrm{B} . \mathrm{O} \cdot=2.5) \end{array}{/tex}
{tex}\begin{array}{cl} \mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow \mathrm{O}_{2}^{+} \\ (\mathrm{B} . \mathrm{O} \cdot=2) \quad(\mathrm{B.O}=2.5) \end{array}{/tex}

questions.explanation: Both the electrons of O2 molecules are in {tex}\pi * 2 p_{y}^{1}=\pi * 2 p_{z}^{1}{/tex} molecular orbitals. Both molecular orbitals have same energy, same shape but different orientation in space.

questions.question: The correct order of bond dissociation energy among N2, O2, {tex}\mathrm{O}^-_{2}{/tex} is shown in which of the following arrangements?
questions.topicName: Bonding In Some Homonuclear Diatomic Molecules
questions.explanation:
The bond order of N2, O2, and {tex}\mathrm{O}^-_{2}{/tex} are 3, 2 and 1.5 respectively.
Since higher bond order implies higher bond dissociation energy, hence the correct order will be N2 > O2 > {tex}\mathrm{O}^-_{2}{/tex}

questions.question: If the magnetic moment of a dioxygen species is 1.73 B.M, it may be:
questions.topicName: Bonding In Some Homonuclear Diatomic Molecules
questions.explanation:
{tex}\mu=\sqrt{n(n+2)}{/tex} B.M.
{tex}1.73=\sqrt{n(n+2)}{/tex}
n = 1
{tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}^{+}=\sigma 1 s^{2} \sigma^{*} 1 s^{2} \sigma 2 s^{2} \sigma^{*} 2 s^{2} \sigma 2 p_{z}^{2} \pi 2 p_{x}^{2}{/tex}
{tex}=\pi 2 p_{y}^{2} \pi^{*} 2 p_{x}^{1}=\pi^{*} 2 p_{y}^{0}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}^{-}=\sigma 1 s^{2} \sigma^{*} 1 s^{2} \sigma 2 s^{2} \sigma^{*} 2 s^{2} \sigma 2 p_{z}^{2} \pi 2 p_{x}^{2}{/tex}
{tex}=\pi 2 p_{y}^{2} \pi^{*} 2 p_{x}^{2}=\pi * 2 p_{y}{ }^{1}{/tex}

questions.question: The bond order and the magnetic characteristics of CN- are:
questions.topicName: Bonding In Some Homonuclear Diatomic Molecules
questions.explanation:
Total number of electrons in CN- = 6 + 7 + 1 = 14
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Molecular orbital distribution
{tex}\sigma l s^{2} \sigma^{*} 1 s^{2} \sigma 2 s^{2} \sigma^{*} 2 s^{2}\left[\begin{array}{l} \pi 2 p x^{2} \\ \pi 2 p y^{2} \end{array}\right] \sigma 2 p_{z}^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Bond order {tex}=\frac{10-4}{2}{/tex} = 3
CN- is diamagnetic because all electrons are paired.

questions.question: Which one of the following properties is not shown by NO?

options.option: It combines with oxygen to form nitrogen dioxide
It is diamagnetic in gaseous state

questions.topicName: Bonding In Some Homonuclear Diatomic Molecules
questions.explanation:
Nitric oxide is paramagnetic in the gaseous state because of the presence of one unpaired electron in its outermost shell. The electronic configuration of NO is
{tex}\sigma \mathrm{KK} \sigma 2 s^{2} \sigma^{*} 2 s^{2} \sigma 2 p_{z}^{2} \pi 2 p^{2}=\pi 2 p_{y}^{2} \pi^{*} 2 p_{x}^{1}=\pi^{*} 2 p_{i}^{0}{/tex}

questions.question: Which one of the following molecules is paramagnetic?
questions.topicName: Bonding In Some Homonuclear Diatomic Molecules
questions.explanation:
The molecular orbital configuration of the molecules given is
Total no. of electrons in NO = 7(N) + 8(O) = 15
Hence E.C. of NO {tex}=\mathrm{KK} \sigma(2 s)^{2} \sigma^{*}(2 s)^{2} \sigma 2 p_{z}^{2} \pi\left(2 p_{x}\right)^{2} \pi\left(2 p_{y}\right)^{2} \pi^{*}\left(2 p_{x}\right)^{1}{/tex}
Due to the presence of one unpaired electron NO is paramagnetic. Except NO all are diamagnetic due to the absence of unpaired electrons.

questions.question: According to molecular orbital theory which of the following statement about the magnetic character and bond order is correct regarding {tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}^{+}{/tex}:
questions.topicName: Bonding In Some Homonuclear Diatomic Molecules
questions.explanation:
{tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}: \sigma 1 s^{2}, \sigma^{*} 1 s^{2}, \sigma 2 s^{2}, \sigma^{*} 2 s^{2}, \sigma 2 p_{x}^{2},{/tex} {tex}\left\{\begin{array}{l} \pi 2 p_{y}^{2} \\ \pi 2 p_{z}^{2} \end{array},\left\{\begin{array}{l} \pi^{*} 2 p_{y}^{1} \\ \pi^{*} 2 p_{z}^{1} \end{array}\right.\right.{/tex}
Bond order {tex}=\frac{10-6}{2}{/tex} = 2
(two unpaired electrons in antibonding molecular orbital)
{tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}^{+}: \sigma 1 s^{2}, \sigma^{*} 1 s^{2}, \sigma 2 s^{2}, \sigma^{*} 2 s^{2}, \sigma 2 p_{x}^{2},\left\{\begin{array}{l} \pi 2 p_{y}^{2},\left\{\pi^{*} 2 p_{y}^{1}\right. \\ \pi 2 p_{z}^{2},\left\{\pi^{*} 2 p_{z}^{0}\right. \end{array}\right.{/tex}
Bond order {tex}=\frac{10-5}{2}{/tex} = 2.5
(One unpaired electron in antibonding molecular orbital)
Hence, O2 as well as {tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}^{+}{/tex} is paramagnetic, and bond order of {tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}^{+}{/tex} is greater than that of O2.

questions.question: Number of paired electrons in O2 molecule is:
questions.topicName: Bonding In Some Homonuclear Diatomic Molecules
questions.explanation:
O2 = Oxygen (Z = 8) has the following molecular orbital configuration of O2.
{tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}\left(16 \mathrm{e}^{-}\right)=\sigma 1 s^{2}, \sigma^{*} 1 s^{2}, \sigma 2 s^{2}, \sigma^{*} 2 s^{2}, \sigma 2 p_{x}^{2},\pi 2 p_{y}^{2} =\pi 2 p_{z}^{2},{/tex} {tex}\pi^{*} 2 p_{y}^{1}=\pi^{*} 2 p_{z}^{1}{/tex} i.e., 2 unpaired and 14 paired electrons.

questions.question:
The molecule having one unpaired electron is:

  1. NO
  2. CO
  3. CN-
  4. O2
questions.topicName: Bonding In Some Homonuclear Diatomic Molecules
questions.explanation:
NOTE THIS STEP: Write the electronic configuration of each species according to molecular orbital theory.
NO (15e-) - {tex}\sigma{/tex}1s2, {tex}\sigma{/tex}*1s2, {tex}\sigma{/tex}2s2, {tex}\sigma{/tex}*2s2, {tex}\sigma 2 p_{x}^{2}{/tex}, {tex}\left\{\pi 2 p_{y}^{2}=\pi 2 p_{z}^{2},\left\{\pi^{*} 2 p_{y}^{1}=\pi^{*} 2 p_{z}^{0}\right.\right.{/tex} 1 unpaired electron.
CO (14e-) - {tex}\sigma{/tex}1s2, {tex}\sigma{/tex}*1s2, {tex}\sigma{/tex}2s2, {tex}\sigma{/tex}*2s2, {tex}\left\{\pi 2 p_{y}^{2}=\pi 2 p_{z}^{2}, \sigma 2 p_{x}^{2}\right.{/tex} no unpaired electron
CN- (14e-) - {tex}\sigma{/tex}1s2, {tex}\sigma{/tex}*1s2, {tex}\sigma{/tex}2s2, {tex}\sigma{/tex}*2s2, {tex}\left\{\pi 2 p_{y}^{2}=\pi 2 p_{z}^{2}, \sigma 2 p_{x}^{2}\right.{/tex}
O2 (16e-) - {tex}\sigma{/tex}1s2, {tex}\sigma{/tex}*1s2, {tex}\sigma{/tex}2s2, {tex}\sigma{/tex}*2s2, {tex}\sigma 2 p_{x}^{2}{/tex}, {tex}\left\{\pi 2 p_{y}^{2}=\pi 2 p_{z}^{2},\left\{\pi^{*} 2 p_{y}{ }^{\prime}=\pi^{*} 2 p_{z}{ }^{1}\right.\right.{/tex}; Two unpaired electrons

questions.question:
Complex A has a composition of H12O6Cl3Cr. If the complex on treatment with conc. H2SO4 loses 13.5% of its original mass, the correct molecular formula of A is:
[Given: atomic mass of Cr = 52 amu and Cl = 35 amu]
questions.topicName: Bonding In Some Homonuclear Diatomic Molecules
questions.explanation:
Conc. H2SO4 acts as a dehydrating agent.
Molar mass of given complex = 266.5 g/mol.
On treating with conc. H2SO4 the mass lost by the complex = {tex}\frac{13.5}{100}{/tex}(266.5) {tex}\approx{/tex} 36 g = 2 moles of H2O
Formula of the complex = [Cr(H2O)4Cl3]Cl {tex}\cdot{/tex} 2H2O

questions.question: Decreasing order of stability of O2, {tex}O_{2}^{-}{/tex}, {tex}O_{2}^{+}{/tex} and {tex}O_{2}^{2-}{/tex} is ________.
questions.topicName: Bonding In Some Homonuclear Diatomic Molecules
questions.explanation:
Species Bond order
O2 2
{tex}O_{2}^{-}{/tex} 1.5
{tex}O_{2}^{+}{/tex} 2.5
{tex}O_{2}^{2-}{/tex} 1

questions.question:
Which of the following statement is INCORRECT from the viewpoint of molecular orbital theory?

  1. Be2 does not exist.
  2. O2, {tex}O_{2}^{-}{/tex}, {tex}O_{3}^{+}{/tex} are all paramagnetic.
  3. Bond strength of N2 is maximum amongst the homonuclear diatomic molecules belonging to the second period.
  4. {tex}N_{2}^{+}{/tex} is diamagnetic.
questions.topicName: Bonding In Some Homonuclear Diatomic Molecules
questions.explanation:
{tex}{N}_{2}^{+}={KK} \sigma 2 {s}^{2} \sigma^{*} 2 {s}^{2} \pi 2 {p}_{k}^{2} \equiv \pi 2 {p}_{v}^{2} \sigma 2 {p}_{x}^{1}{/tex}
Due to presence of one unpaired electron, {tex}N_{2}^{+}{/tex} is paramagnetic.

questions.question: The number of electrons in {tex}\pi{/tex}2py orbital of O2 is a, in {tex}\pi{/tex}2py orbital of B2 is b, in {tex}\pi{/tex}2px orbital of C2 is c and in {tex}\sigma{/tex}2pz orbital of N2 is d. The sum of (a + b + c + d) is ________.
questions.topicName: Bonding In Some Homonuclear Diatomic Molecules
questions.explanation:
O2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\pi{/tex}2py {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2e-
B2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\pi{/tex}2py {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1e-
C2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\pi{/tex}2px {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2e-
N2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sigma{/tex}2pz {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2e-
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Sum = 2 + 1 + 2 + 2 = 7

questions.question: The bond order in NO+ molecule is ________.
questions.topicName: Bonding In Some Homonuclear Diatomic Molecules
questions.explanation:
Electronic configuration of NO+ molecule is
NO+ : {tex}\begin{array}{llll} {KK} (\sigma 2 {s})^{2} \left(\sigma^{*} 2{s}\right)^{2} \left(\sigma 2 {p}_{2}\right)^{2} \left(\pi 2 {p}_{x}\right)^{2} \end{array}{/tex}
{tex}\equiv\left(\pi 2 {p}_{\nu}\right)^{2}\left(\pi^{*} 2 {p}_{{x}}\right)^{0}{/tex}
KK represents closed K shell structure {tex}(\sigma 1 s)^{2}\left(\sigma^{*} 1 s\right)^{2}{/tex}
Bond order of NO+ molecule = {tex}\frac{{N}_{{b}}-{N}_{{a}}}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{8-2}{2}{/tex} = 3

questions.question: How many ions of the following have bond order of 2.5? {tex}N_{2}^{-}{/tex}, NO-, {tex}C_{2}^{-}{/tex}, {tex}N_{2}^{+}{/tex}, {tex}C_{2}^{2-}{/tex}, CN+
questions.topicName: Bonding In Some Homonuclear Diatomic Molecules
questions.explanation:
Species Bond order
{tex}N_{2}^{-}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}(8 - 3) = 2.5
NO- {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}(8 - 4) = 2
{tex}C_{2}^{-}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}(7 - 2) = 2.5
{tex}N_{2}^{+}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}(7 - 2) = 2.5
{tex}C_{2}^{2-}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}(8 - 2) = 3
CN+ {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}(6 - 2) = 2

questions.question:
Three statements are given below:

  2. {tex}NO_{3}^{-}{/tex} is a linear molecule.
  3. The F-B-F bond angle in BF3 is 120o.
    From the above
questions.topicName: Bonding In Some Homonuclear Diatomic Molecules
questions.explanation: {tex}NO_{3}^{-}{/tex} is a trigonal planar molecule.

questions.question: The bond order of {tex}{He}_{2}^{-}{/tex} molecule is ________.
questions.topicName: Bonding In Some Homonuclear Diatomic Molecules
questions.explanation:
{tex}{He}_{2}^{-}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\sigma 1 {s}^{2} \sigma^{*} 1 {s}^{2} \sigma 2 {s}^{1}{/tex}
B.O = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}(NB - NA)
= {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}(3 - 2)
= 0.5

questions.question: Which of the following diatomic molecular species has only {tex}\pi{/tex} bonds according to molecular orbital theory?
questions.topicName: Bonding In Some Homonuclear Diatomic Molecules
questions.explanation:
C2 : {tex}\sigma 1 {s}^{2} \sigma^{*} 1 {s}^{2} \sigma 2 {s}^{2} \sigma^{*} 2 {s}^{2} \pi 2 {p}_{{x}}^{2}=\pi 2 {py}^{2}{/tex}
Hence, according to molecular orbital theory, the orbitals involved in bonding are only {tex}\pi{/tex} orbitals ({tex}\pi{/tex}2px, {tex}\pi{/tex}2py).

questions.question: Which of the following options represents the CORRECT bond order?
questions.topicName: Bonding In Some Homonuclear Diatomic Molecules
questions.explanation:
Bond order = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}(Nb - Na)
Electronic configuration of O2 can be given as:
{tex}\sigma 1 \mathrm{~s}^{2} \sigma^{*} 1 \mathrm{~s}^{2} \sigma 2 \mathrm{~s}^{2} \sigma^{*} 2 \mathrm{~s}^{2} \sigma 2 \mathrm{p}_{z}^{2} \pi 2 \mathrm{p}_{x}^{2}\equiv\pi 2 \mathrm{p}_{y}^{2} \pi^{*} 2 \mathrm{p}_{x}{ }^{1} {/tex}{tex}\equiv \pi^* 2 \mathrm{p}_{y}{ }^{1}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Bond order = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}(10 - 6) = 2
Electronic configuration of {tex}{O}_{2}^{+}{/tex} can be given as:
{tex}\sigma 1 \mathrm{~s}^{2} \sigma^{*} 1 \mathrm{~s}^{2} \sigma 2 \mathrm{~s}^{2} \sigma^{*} 2 \mathrm{~s}^{2} \sigma 2 \mathrm{p}_{z}^{2} \pi 2 \mathrm{p}_{x}^{2}\equiv\pi 2 \mathrm{p}_{y}^{2} \ \ \pi^{*} 2 \mathrm{p}_{x}{ }^{1} {/tex}{tex}\equiv \pi^{*} 2 \mathrm{p}_{y}^{0}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Bond order = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}(10 - 5) = 2.5
Electronic configuration of {tex}{O}_{2}^{-}{/tex} can be given as:
{tex}\sigma 1 \mathrm{~s}^{2} \sigma^{*} 1 \mathrm{~s}^{2} \sigma 2 \mathrm{~s}^{2} \sigma^{*} 2 \mathrm{~s}^{2} \sigma 2 \mathrm{p}_{z}^{2} \pi 2 \mathrm{p}_{x}^{2}\equiv\pi 2 \mathrm{p}_{y}^{2} \ \ \pi^{*} 2 \mathrm{p}_{x}{ }^{2}{/tex} {tex}\equiv \pi^{*} 2 \mathrm{p}_{y}^{1}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Bond order = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}(10 - 7) = 1.5
Thus, bond order is {tex}{O}_{2}^{-}<{O}_{2}<{O}_{2}^{+}{/tex}

questions.question:
Consider the following species: CN+, CN-, NO, CN
Which one of these will have the highest bond order?
questions.topicName: Bonding In Some Homonuclear Diatomic Molecules
questions.explanation:
Species Bond order
NO {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}(10 - 5) = 2.5
CN- {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}(10 - 4) = 3
CN+ {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}(8 - 4) = 2
CN {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}(9 - 4) = 2.5

questions.question: Which one of the following pairs of species have the same bond order?
questions.topicName: Bonding In Some Homonuclear Diatomic Molecules
questions.explanation: Isoelectronic and isostructural species have same bond order.

questions.question:
Find the INCORRECT match:

  Characteristics Order
i. Bond length {tex}{O}_{2}^{2-}>{O}_{2}^{-}>{O}_{2}^{+}>{O}_{2}{/tex}
ii. Bond dissociation energy {tex}{O}_{2}^{2-}>{O}_{2}^{-}>{O}_{2}>{O}_{2}^{+}{/tex}
iii. Relative stability {tex}{O}_{2}^{+}>{O}_{2}>{O}_{2}^{-}>{O}_{2}^{2-}{/tex}
questions.topicName: Bonding In Some Homonuclear Diatomic Molecules
questions.explanation:
Correct combinations:

  1. Bond length: Bond length is inversely proportional to bond order. Bond length order is {tex}{O}_{2}^{2-}>{O}_{2}^{-}>{O}_{2}>{O}_{2}^{+}{/tex}
  2. Bond dissociation energy: Bond dissociation energy is directly proportional to bond order. Therefore, the dissociation energy is in the order, {tex}{O}_{2}^{+}>{O}_{2}>{O}_{2}^{-}>{O}_{2}^{2-}{/tex}
  3. Greater the bond dissociation energy, greater is stability {tex}{O}_{2}^{+}>{O}_{2}>{O}_{2}^- >{O}_{2}^{2-}{/tex}

i - b, ii - d, iii - a, iv - c

questions.topicName: Bonding In Some Homonuclear Diatomic Molecules
questions.explanation: i - c, ii - d, iii - a, iv - b

questions.question: The bond order and magnetic property of acetylide ion are same as that of
questions.topicName: Bonding In Some Homonuclear Diatomic Molecules

Acetylide ion {tex}\rightarrow \mathrm{C}_2^{2-} {/tex} {tex}(\overline{\mathrm{C}} \equiv \overline{\mathrm{C}}){/tex}

questions.question: What is the number of unpaired electron(s) in the highest occupied molecular orbital of the following species: N2; {tex}\mathrm{N}_2^{+}{/tex}; O2; {tex}\mathrm{O}_2^{+} {/tex}?
questions.topicName: Bonding In Some Homonuclear Diatomic Molecules
questions.explanation:
{tex}\mathrm{N}_2 \sigma \mathrm{s}^2 \sigma^* 1 \mathrm{~s}^2 \sigma 2 \mathrm{~s}^2 \sigma^* 2 \mathrm{~s}^2\left(\pi 2 \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{x}}^2=\pi 2 \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{y}}^2\right) \frac{\sigma 2 \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{z}}^2}{\mathrm{HOMO}}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{N}_2^{+}-\sigma 1 \mathrm{~s}^2 \sigma^* 1 \mathrm{~s}^2 \sigma^* 2 \mathrm{~s}^2 \sigma^* 2 \mathrm{~s}^2\left(\pi 2 \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{x}}^2=\pi 2 \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{y}}^2\right) \frac{\sigma 2 \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{z}}^1}{\text { HOMO }}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{O}_2-\sigma 1 \mathrm{~s}^2 \sigma^* 1 \mathrm{~s}^2 \sigma 2 \mathrm{~s}^2 \sigma^* 2 \mathrm{~s}^2 \sigma 2 \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{z}}^2\left(\pi 2 \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{x}}^2=\pi 2 \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{y}}^2\right){/tex}
{tex}\underset{HOMO}{\left(\pi^\ast2\mathrm p_{\mathrm x}^1=\pi^\ast2\mathrm p_{\mathrm y}^1\right)}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{O}_2^{+}-\sigma 1 \mathrm{~s}^2 \sigma^* 1 \mathrm{~s}^2 \sigma 2 \mathrm{~s}^2 \sigma^* 2 \mathrm{~s}^2 \sigma 2 \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{z}}^2\left(\pi 2 \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{x}}^2=\pi 2 \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{y}}^2\right){/tex}
{tex}\underset{HOMO}{\left(\pi\ast2p_{\mathrm x}^1=\pi^\ast2\mathrm p_{\mathrm y}^0\right)}{/tex}
N2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 0 unpaired e- in HOMO
{tex}\mathrm{N}_2^{+}{/tex}  {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 unpaired e- in HOMO
O2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 unpaired e- in HOMO
{tex}\mathrm{O}_2^{+} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 unpaired e- in HOMO

questions.question: Hydrogen bonds are formed in many compounds e.g., H2O, HF, NH3. The boiling point of such compounds depends to a large extent on the strength of the hydrogen bond and the number of hydrogen bonds. The correct decreasing order of the boiling points of the above compounds is:
questions.topicName: Hydrogen Bonding and its Applications
questions.explanation:
The smaller the size of the atom, the greater is the electronegativity, and hence stronger is the H−bonding. Thus, the order of strength of H-bonding is HF > H2O > NH3. 
But each HF molecule is linked only to two other HF molecules while each H2​O molecule is linked to four other H2​O molecules through H−bonding.
Hence, the decreasing order of boiling points is H2​O > HF > NH3.

questions.question: Out of the following, intramolecular hydrogen bonding exists in.
questions.topicName: Hydrogen Bonding and its Applications
questions.explanation:
If a hydrogen bond occurs within two functional group of the same molecule, it is called an intramolecular hydrogen bond. This occurs when the hydrogen donor and the acceptor are both within the same molecule.
In the 2-nitro phenol molecule, the O atom in the –OH group is more electronegative than the H and hence δ-. H atom, on the other hand, is δ+. Therefore, the O atom in the –OH group acts as the H donor whereas O atom on the nitro group acts as the H-acceptor.

{tex}...\mathop H\limits^{\delta + } - \mathop F\limits^{\delta - } ....\mathop H\limits^{\delta + } - \mathop F\limits^{\delta - } ....\mathop H\limits^{\delta + } - \mathop F\limits^{\delta - } {/tex}
In this molecule, hydrogen bond behaves like a bridge between two atoms that holds one atom by covalent bond and the other by hydrogen bond.

In {tex}{H_2}S{/tex} , those bonds don't exist, because sulfur is much less electronegative.

questions.question: Which of the following is the weakest acid?
questions.topicName: Hydrogen Bonding and its Applications
questions.explanation:
HF is the weakest acid because of its strong H-F bond. Fluorine being small in size overlaps better with 1s orbital of hydrogen leading to a strong bond. Hence can not give proton easily. Here bond strength overweighs the electronegativity of F.
As we go down the group, the size of the halogens increases. This results in a poor overlap of its orbital with 1s  orbital of hydrogen making the H-X bond weaker. H-I bond is the weakest of all, so it is the strongest acid.

questions.question:
The increasing order of the boiling points for the following compounds is:

  1. C2H5OH
  2. C2H5Cl
  3. C2H5CH3
  4. C2H5OCH3
questions.topicName: Hydrogen Bonding and its Applications
questions.explanation:
1. C2H5OH Hydrogen bonding 
    Hydrogen bonding > dipole-dipole
  2. C2H5Cl Dipole-dipole
    C2H5Cl is more polar than C2H5CH3 so, dipole-dipole C2H5Cl > dipole-dipole C2H5OCH3
  3. C2H5CH3 Weak van der Waals forces
  4. C2H5OCH3 Dipole-dipole

Thus, boiling point order is:
(III) < (IV) < (II) < (I)

questions.question:
Which of the following is NOT an effect of hydrogen bonding?

  1. Existence of KHF2.
  2. Ice floating on water.
  3. Molecular mass of acetic acid in benzene is 120 g mol-1.
  4. Bond angle in NH3 is greater than NF3.
questions.topicName: Hydrogen Bonding and its Applications
questions.explanation:
1. In aqueous solution, hydrogen fluoride dissociates to give the difluoride ion (HF2), instead of fluoride ion (F-). This is due to H-bonding in HF which explains the existence of KHF2.
  2. In solid ice, hydrogen bonding gives rise to a cage-like structure made up of water molecules. This structure has larger volume and lower density. Hence, ice floats on water.
  3. In benzene, molecules of acetic acid exist as dimers due to hydrogen bonding. Hence, its molecular mass is found to be two times of those calculated from its simple molecular formula.

questions.question:
Water is called the universal solvent as it dissolves many substances in it. The most probable reasons are:

  1. It is a polar molecule
  2. It’s density is low in solid state than in liquid state.
  3. It has high boiling point.
  4. It has the ability to form H-bond with each other and with other molecules.
questions.topicName: Hydrogen Bonding and its Applications
questions.explanation:
1. It is a polar molecule.
  2. It has the ability to form H-bond with each other and with other molecules.

questions.question: The correct order of increasing intermolecular hydrogen bond strength is
questions.topicName: Hydrogen Bonding and its Applications
questions.explanation:
The strength of H-bonding depends upon two factors:

  1. Electronegativity difference between the hydrogen and Halogen element.
  2. Number of H-bonding interaction.

So the order of H-bonding is CH4 < HCN < NH3.

questions.question: Which one of the following compounds shows the presence of intramolecular hydrogen bond?
questions.topicName: Hydrogen Bonding and its Applications
questions.explanation: Cellulose is a polymer of glucose which are interlinked to each other. The chain of cellulose is stabilized by two hydrogen bond parallel to the glycosidic linkage. Hydrogen bonding it is considered as intramolecular hydrogen bonding. While H2O2, HCN and CH3COOH form intermolecular hydrogen bonding.

questions.question: Which one of the following molecules will form a linear polymeric structure due to hydrogen bonding?

questions.explanation:
NH3 and H2O are non-linear molecules. Among HCI and HF, a linear polymeric structure is formed by HF molecules shown as follows:
{tex}\mathrm H-\mathrm F\;...\;\mathrm H-\mathrm F\;\underset{\underset{\mathrm{Hydrogen}\;\mathrm{bonding}}\downarrow}{...}\;\mathrm H-\mathrm F\;...\;\mathrm H-\mathrm F{/tex}

options.option: There is intermolecular hydrogen bonding in p-nitrophenol

Intramolecular hydrogen bonding exists in p-nitrophenol
p-nitrophenol has a higher molecular weight

questions.topicName: Hydrogen Bonding and its Applications
questions.explanation: There is intermolecular hydrogen bonding in p-nitrophenol which binds the molecules together to increase the boiling point as compared to o-nitrophenol in which intramolecular hydrogen bonding predominates.
